7.31371 10 4V0H11V4Z" fill="var(--border-faint)"></path></svg><svg fill="none" height="11" viewBox="0 0 21 11" width="21" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" class="pointer-events-none contain-[layout,paint] absolute absolute bottom-0 -left-10 z-[1]"><path d="M11 4C11 7.31371 13.6863 10 17 10H21V11H0V10H4C7.31371 10 10 7.31371 10 4V0H11V4Z" fill="var(--border-faint)"></path></svg><div class="p-32 bg-black/1 relative overflow-x-auto overflow-y-auto max-h-400"><pre tabindex="0" data-language="bash" data-theme="css-variables" class="font-mono text-mono-medium"><code data-language="bash" data-theme="css-variables" style="display:grid" class="font-mono text-mono-medium"><span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)">git</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string)"> add</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string)"> .</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)">git</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string)"> commit</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string)"> -m</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)"> &quot;Add a workflow to scrape on a schedule&quot;</span></span></code></pre></div></div></div></figure>
<p>Once you do, the workflow must show up in the Actions tab of your repository like below:</p>
<p><img src="/images/blog/scheduling-scrapers-images/actions.png" alt="&quot;GitHub Actions workflow showing successful execution of scheduled web scraping with Firecrawl&quot;"/></p>
<p>Click on the workflow name and press the &quot;Run workflow&quot; button. This launches the action manually and starts the schedule. If you check in after some time, you should see more automatic runs and the results persisted in your repository:</p>
<p><img src="/images/blog/scheduling-scrapers-images/finished_actions.png" alt="&quot;GitHub Actions workflow showing multiple successful executions of scheduled web scraping with Firecrawl over a 24 hour period&quot;"/></p>
<p>Caution: I left the workflow running overnight (at five minute intervals) and was nastily surprised by 96 workflow runs the next day. Thankfully, GitHub actions are free (up to 2000 min/month) unlike AWS instances.</p>
<p>Now, unless you disable the workflow manually by clicking the three dots in the upper-right corner, the scraper continues running on the schedule you specified.</p>
<div class="!text-title-h5 [&amp;:not(:first-child)]:mt-72 mb-24 relative"><span class="absolute -top-[120px]" id="best-practices-and-optimization"></span><h2 class="!text-title-h5">Best Practices and Optimization</h2></div>
<p>When scheduling web scrapers, following best practices ensures reliability, efficiency, and ethical behavior. Here are the key areas to consider:</p>
<div class="blog-heading relative [&amp;:not(:first-child)]:mt-48 mb-16"><span class="absolute -top-[120px]" id="1.-rate-limiting-and-delays"></span><h3>1. Rate limiting and delays</h3></div>
<p>Scraping engines like Firecrawl usually come with built-in rate limiting. However, if you are using custom scrapers written with Python libraries, you must always respect website servers by implementing proper rate limiting and delay strategies. For example, the below example shows adding random delays between your requests in-between requests to respect server load and avoid getting your IP blocked:</p>
<figure data-rehype-pretty-code-figure=""><div class="relative"><div class="absolute top-0 left-[calc(50%-50vw)] z-[1] w-screen h-1 bg-border-faint"><div class="cmw-container absolute top-0 left-0 class="font-mono text-mono-medium"><code data-language="python" data-theme="css-variables" style="display:grid" class="font-mono text-mono-medium"><span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">import</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)"> time</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">import</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)"> random</span></span>
<span data-line=""> </span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">def</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)"> scrape_with_delays</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">(</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-parameter)">urls</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">):</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">    for</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)"> url </span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">in</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)"> urls</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">:</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">        try</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">:</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-comment)">            # Random delay between 2-5 seconds</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">            delay </span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">=</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)"> random</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">.</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)">uniform</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">(</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)">2</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">, </span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)">5</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">)</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">            time</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">.</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)">sleep</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">(delay)</span></span>
<span data-line=""> </span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-comment)">            # Your scraping code here</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">            response </span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">=</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)"> requests</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">.</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)">get</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">(url)</span></span>
<span data-line=""> </span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">        except</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)"> requests</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">.</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">RequestException </span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">as</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)"> e</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">:</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">            logging</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">.</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)">error</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">(</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">f</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">&quot;Error scraping </span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)">{</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">url</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)">}</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">: </span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)">{</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">e</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)">}</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">&quot;</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">)</span></span></code></pre></div></div></div></figure>
<p>Best practices for rate limiting:</p>
<ul>
<li>Add random delays between requests (2-5 seconds minimum)</li>
<li>Respect <code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">robots.txt</code> directives</li>
<li>Implement exponential backoff for retries</li>
<li>Stay under 1 request per second for most sites</li>
<li>Monitor response headers for rate limit information</li>
</ul>
<div class="blog-heading relative [&amp;:not(:first-child)]:mt-48 mb-16"><span class="absolute -top-[120px]" id="2.-proxy-rotation"></span><h3>2. Proxy Rotation</h3></div>
<p>This best practice is related to using custom web scrapers. Proxy rotation involves cycling through different IP addresses when making requests to avoid getting blocked. By distributing requests across multiple IPs, you can maintain access to websites that might otherwise flag high-volume traffic from a single source.</p>
<figure data-rehype-pretty-code-figure=""><div class="relative"><div class="absolute top-0 left-[calc(50%-50vw)] z-[1] w-screen h-1 bg-border-faint"><div class="cmw-container absolute top-0 left-0 fill="var(--border-faint)"></path></svg><div class="p-32 bg-black/1 relative overflow-x-auto overflow-y-auto max-h-400"><pre tabindex="0" data-language="python" data-theme="css-variables" class="font-mono text-mono-medium"><code data-language="python" data-theme="css-variables" style="display:grid" class="font-mono text-mono-medium"><span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">from</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)"> itertools </span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">import</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)"> cycle</span></span>
<span data-line=""> </span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">def</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)"> get_proxy_pool</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">():</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">    proxies </span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">=</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)"> [</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">        &#x27;http://proxy1:port&#x27;</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">,</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">        &#x27;http://proxy2:port&#x27;</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">,</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">        &#x27;http://proxy3:port&#x27;</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">    ]</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">    return</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)"> cycle</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">(proxies)</span></span>
<span data-line=""> </span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">def</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)"> scrape_with_proxies</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">(</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-parameter)">url</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">,</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-parameter)"> proxy_pool</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">):</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">    for</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)"> _ </span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">in</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)"> range</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">(</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)">3</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">):</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-comment)">  # Max 3 retries</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">        try</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">:</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">            proxy </span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">=</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)"> next</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">(proxy_pool)</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">            response </span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">=</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)"> requests</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">.</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)">get</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">(</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">                url,</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">                proxies</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">=</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">{</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">&#x27;http&#x27;</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">: proxy, </span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">&#x27;https&#x27;</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">: proxy},</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">                timeout</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">=</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)">10</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">            )</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">            return</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)"> response</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">        except</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)"> requests</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">.</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">RequestException</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">:</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">            continue</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">    return</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)"> None</span></span></code></pre></div></div></div></figure>
<p>Proxy best practices:</p>
<ul>
<li>Rotate IPs regularly</li>
<li>Use high-quality proxy services</li>
<li>Implement timeout handling</li>
<li>Monitor proxy health</li>
<li>Keep backup proxies ready</li>
</ul>
<div class="blog-heading relative [&amp;:not(:first-child)]:mt-48 mb-16"><span class="absolute -top-[120px]" id="3.-data-storage-strategies"></span><h3>3. Data Storage Strategies</h3></div>
<p>The example scrapers we have built today saved the data to a JSON file, which is a simple method. Based on your needs, you may consider building a full data storage solution that saves the scraped data to various formats like as CSV files or to an SQL database. The storage type always depends on the kind of information scraped. Below is an example class that can save lists of dictionaries (like we scraped today) to a CSV file:</p>

<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">        elif</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)"> format</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)"> ==</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)"> &#x27;json&#x27;</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">:</span></span>

<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">            with</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)"> open</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">(filename, </span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-string-expression)">&#x27;w&#x27;</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">)</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)"> as</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)"> f</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">:</span></span>

<span data-line=""> </span>

<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">        self</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">.</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)">_cleanup_old_files</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">(days</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">=</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-constant)">7</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">)</span></span>
<span data-line=""> </span>

<span data-line=""> </span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">    def</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-function)"> _cleanup_old_files</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">(</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-parameter)">self</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-punctuation)">,</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-parameter)"> days</span><span style="color:var(--shiki-foreground)">):</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-comment)">        # Implementation for cleaning up old files</span></span>
<span data-line=""><span style="color:var(--shiki-token-keyword)">        pass</span></span></code></pre></div></div></div></figure>
<p>Storage recommendations:</p>
<ul>
<li>Use appropriate file formats (CSV/JSON/Database)</li>
<li>Implement data versioning</li>
<li>Regular cleanup of old data</li>
<li>Compress historical data</li>
<li>Consider using a database for large datasets</li>
</ul>
<div class="blog-heading relative [&amp;:not(:first-child)]:mt-48 mb-16"><span class="absolute -top-[120px]" id="4.-error-logging-and-monitoring"></span><h3>4. Error Logging and Monitoring</h3></div>

<p>Monitoring best practices:</p>
<ul>
<li>Implement comprehensive logging</li>
<li>Set up alerts for critical failures</li>
<li>Monitor memory usage</li>
<li>Track success rates</li>
<li>Log response times</li>
</ul>
<div class="blog-heading relative [&amp;:not(:first-child)]:mt-48 mb-16"><span class="absolute -top-[120px]" id="5.-maintaining-your-scraper"></span><h3>5. Maintaining Your Scraper</h3></div>
<p>Maintaining your web scraper is crucial for ensuring reliable and continuous data collection. Web scraping targets are dynamic - websites frequently update their structure, implement new security measures, or change their content organization. Regular maintenance helps catch these changes early, prevents scraping failures, and ensures your data pipeline remains robust. A well-maintained scraper also helps manage resources efficiently, keeps code quality high, and adapts to evolving requirements.</p>
<p>Maintenance guidelines:</p>
<ul>
<li>Regular code updates</li>
<li>Monitor site changes</li>
<li>Update user agents periodically</li>
<li>Check for library updates</li>
<li>Implement health checks</li>
<li>Document maintenance procedures</li>
</ul>
<div class="!text-title-h5 [&amp;:not(:first-child)]:mt-72 mb-24 relative"><span class="absolute -top-[120px]" id="conclusion"></span><h2 class="!text-title-h5">Conclusion</h2></div>
<p>Throughout this guide, we&#x27;ve explored how to effectively schedule web scrapers using local Python and operation system tools as well as GitHub actions. From basic setup to advanced optimization techniques, we&#x27;ve covered the essential components needed to build reliable, automated data collection pipelines. The workflow we&#x27;ve created not only handles the technical aspects of scheduling but also incorporates best practices for rate limiting, error handling, and data storage - crucial elements for any production-grade scraping system.</p>
<p>For those looking to enhance their web scraping capabilities further, I recommend exploring Firecrawl&#x27;s comprehensive features through their <a class="!underline" href="https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/mastering-the-crawl-endpoint-in-firecrawl">/crawl endpoint guide</a> and <a class="!underline" href="https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/mastering-firecrawl-scrape-endpoint">/scrape endpoint tutorial</a>. These resources, along with the <a class="!underline" href="https://docs.firecrawl.dev">official documentation</a>, provide deeper insights into advanced topics like JavaScript rendering, structured data extraction, and batch operations that can significantly improve your web scraping workflows. Whether you&#x27;re building training datasets for AI models or monitoring websites for changes, combining scheduled scraping with these powerful tools can help you build more sophisticated and efficient data collection systems.</p>
<div class="!text-title-h5 [&amp;:not(:first-child)]:mt-72 mb-24 relative"><span class="absolute -top-[120px]" id="frequently-asked-questions"></span><h2 class="!text-title-h5">Frequently Asked Questions</h2></div>
<p><strong>1. What&#x27;s the best way to schedule web scrapers in Python?</strong></p>
<p>For local scheduling, Python&#x27;s <code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">schedule</code> library offers the simplest setup with intuitive syntax. For production use, cron jobs (Unix/macOS) or Windows Task Scheduler provide more reliability. Cloud-based GitHub Actions ensures scrapers run even when your local machine is off.</p>
<p><strong>2. Why should I use Firecrawl instead of traditional scraping libraries?</strong></p>
<p>Firecrawl uses AI-powered semantic extraction instead of brittle HTML selectors, meaning your scrapers adapt automatically to website changes. Traditional libraries like BeautifulSoup require constant maintenance when sites update their structure, while Firecrawl maintains reliability without manual intervention.</p>
<p><strong>3. How often should I run my scheduled web scraper?</strong></p>
<p>Schedule frequency depends on data freshness needs and website update patterns. For real-time data like stock prices, run every few minutes. For daily content updates, hourly or daily schedules work well. Always implement rate limiting and respect robots.txt to avoid server overload.</p>
<p><strong>4. What&#x27;s the difference between local and cloud-based scraper scheduling?</strong></p>
<p>Local scheduling (cron, Task Scheduler, Python schedule) requires your machine to stay on and connected. Cloud-based solutions like GitHub Actions run on remote servers, providing better uptime and reliability. GitHub Actions offers 2000 free minutes monthly, making it cost-effective for most projects.</p>
<p><strong>5. How do I prevent my scheduled scraper from getting blocked?</strong></p>
<p>Implement random delays between requests (2-5 seconds minimum), respect robots.txt directives, use proxy rotation, and stay under 1 request per second. Firecrawl handles many anti-bot measures automatically, while custom scrapers require manual implementation of these protections.</p>
<p><strong>6. What&#x27;s the best way to store scraped data from scheduled jobs?</strong></p>
<p>Use timestamped JSON or CSV files for simple projects, PostgreSQL or MongoDB for larger datasets requiring queries, and implement automatic cleanup of old files. Consider data compression for historical records and maintain proper versioning for tracking changes over time.</p>
<p><strong>7. How do I monitor and debug scheduled web scrapers?</strong></p>
<p>Set up comprehensive logging using Python&#x27;s logging module with both file and console handlers. Track success rates, response times, and errors. For production scrapers, implement health checks and alerts for critical failures. GitHub Actions provides built-in logs for cloud-based scrapers.</p>
<p><strong>8. Can I schedule multiple web scrapers to run concurrently?</strong></p>
<p>Yes, use Python&#x27;s <code class="font-mono text-mono-medium">asyncio</code> for concurrent execution of multiple scrapers with different intervals. This approach provides true concurrency, precise timing control, and better resource efficiency than running scrapers sequentially. GitHub Actions also supports multiple scheduled workflows running independently.</p></div></div></div><div class="h-42 p-30 border-t border-border-faint relative lg-max:hidden"><svg fill="none" structured web data into your app through API calls instead of building scraping pipelines\n- Four patterns: search + scrape in one call, structured extraction from known URLs, multi-page crawling, and agent-driven gathering\n- Code examples use Firecrawl's Python SDK and run as-is\n- Best practices for picking the right pattern and managing costs\n\n---\n\nYour product manager wants a feature that pulls live data from the web. Could be a research assistant, competitor monitoring, or an \"import from URL\" button. You start building and quickly realize the difference between \"search the web\" and \"get structured data my LLM can use\" is bigger than you thought. By the time you've wired up a search API, a scraper, and some parsing logic, you've built a fragile pipeline before writing any product code.\n\nThere are APIs now that cover most of that stack, and this tutorial walks through four patterns using Firecrawl's Python SDK.\n\n## What is AI-powered search / data retrieval?\n\nAI-powered data retrieval uses language models behind API calls to search the web, read pages, and return structured data. You describe what you need or point to a URL, and the API sends back markdown or typed JSON instead of raw HTML.\n\n![Diagram comparing three generations of web data retrieval from HTML scrapers to SERP APIs to AI-powered data retrieval APIs](/images/blog/ai-powered-data-retrieval/web_data_generations.webp)\n\nFor years, getting web data meant writing scrapers. You'd dig through a page's HTML to find and grab the elements you need, then figure out how to clean the output into something usable. It worked when sites held still, but one layout change could break a pipeline overnight. At scale, scraper maintenance became its own project.\n\nSERP APIs made the search part easier. Services like SerpAPI or Serper return structured JSON for any query: titles, URLs, short snippets. But that's still metadata about pages, not the actual content your LLM needs.\n\nA newer set of APIs delivers the content too. You send a query or point to a URL, and you get back markdown, JSON, or structured data matching a schema you define. All the fetching and formatting happens on the API side, which is what makes these APIs a good fit for products that need web data without the overhead of maintaining a scraping pipeline.\n\n## AI-powered search trends in 2026\n\nMicrosoft retired the [Bing Search APIs](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/bing-search-api-alternatives) in August 2025. If you were building on Bing, you got pushed toward Azure AI Agents and 'Grounding with Bing Search,' which is both pricier and more locked-in. A lot of developers moved to independent providers instead, and that accelerated the shift toward [AI-native search APIs](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/top_web_search_api_2025).\n\nAround the same time, RAG stopped being experimental. By early 2026, most AI products that need current information run some form of [retrieval-augmented generation](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/best-chunking-strategies-rag), which means more developers need APIs that return content they can feed to a model, not just links. SerpAPI, Serper, and Brave stop at search metadata. If you're using Firecrawl, Exa, or Tavily, you get the page content in the same call and skip the scraping step.\n\nThe newer development is agent-driven gathering. Instead of spelling out exactly where to look, you describe what you need in a prompt and the API figures out the sourcing. That makes things like competitive analysis, lead enrichment, and market monitoring a lot more practical to automate.\n\nThe shift toward RAG means that we need a strong content layer. Links and snippets aren't enough when your model needs to read the page. Firecrawl's four endpoints — search, scrape, crawl, and agent — are all built around returning content your LLM can use, not just pointers to it\n\n## How to add AI-powered search to your product\n\nFirecrawl's Python SDK covers these retrieval patterns through four endpoints:\n\n- `/search` finds pages and optionally scrapes their full content in one call\n- `/scrape` converts a known URL to markdown or structured JSON using a schema\n- `/crawl` does the same across multiple linked pages on a site\n- `/agent` takes a prompt, finds its own sources, and returns structured data\n\n![Pipeline showing how Firecrawl search scrape crawl and agent endpoints compose together for AI-powered vendor evaluation data retrieval](/images/blog/ai-powered-data-retrieval/vendor_evaluation_pipeline.webp)\n\nThe examples below use a vendor evaluation project (comparing email API providers like Resend, SendGrid, Postmark) to show all four working together, but each pattern applies independently to any feature that needs web data.\n\n### Setup\n\n```python\npip install firecrawl-py pydantic\n```\n\n```python\nfrom firecrawl import Firecrawl\nfrom firecrawl.v2.types import ScrapeOptions\nfrom pydantic import BaseModel, Field\nfrom typing import List\n\napp = Firecrawl(api_key=\"fc-YOUR_API_KEY\")\n```\n\nGrab your API key from [firecrawl.dev](https://www.firecrawl.dev). The free tier covers everything in this tutorial.\n\n### Search and scrape with /search\n\nWhen you need to find relevant pages and get their content in one step, [/search](https://docs.firecrawl.dev/features/search) does both. It runs a web search and scrapes each result, returning full page markdown instead of a list of links.\n\n```python\nresults = app.search(\n    \"best transactional email API providers 2026\",\n    limit=5,\n    scrape_options=ScrapeOptions(formats=[\"markdown\"]),\n)\n\nfor doc in results.web:\n    if hasattr(doc, 'metadata'):\n        print(doc.metadata.title)\n        print(f\"  {doc.metadata.url}\")\n        print(f\"  {len(doc.markdown)} chars of content\")\n    else:\n        print(doc.title)\n        print(f\"  {doc.url}\")\n        print(f\"  (no content scraped)\")\n    print()\n```\n\n```\n13 Best Transactional Email Services (2026)\n  https://www.emailtooltester.com/en/blog/best-transactional-email-service/\n  42,318 chars of content\n\nThe 8 Best Email APIs Compared\n  https://www.courier.com/blog/best-email-apis/\n  28,104 chars of content\n```\n\nEach result is a `Document` with the page's full markdown and metadata (title, URL, status code). Without `scrape_options`, you'd get back `SearchResultWeb` objects with just titles, URLs, and short descriptions, which is what traditional SERP APIs return. Even with scrape_options, some results may come back as SearchResultWeb if a page fails to scrape. Check for metadata before accessing it to avoid an AttributeError.\n\nThe `scrape_options` parameter takes a `ScrapeOptions` instance imported from `firecrawl.v2.types`, not a plain dict (this trips people up on first use).\n\nCost: 2 credits per 10 search results, plus 1 credit per page scraped.\n\n### Structured extraction with /scrape\n\nMarkdown is useful for feeding content to an LLM, but some features need typed fields you can store and compare. The [/scrape endpoint's](https://docs.firecrawl.dev/features/scrape) JSON extraction mode does that: you point it at a URL with a Pydantic schema, and it returns structured data matching your schema.\n\n```python\nclass PricingTier(BaseModel):\n    name: str = Field(description=\"Tier name, e.g. Free, Pro, Enterprise\")\n    price: str = Field(description=\"Monthly price\")\n    email_limit: str = Field(description=\"Emails included per month\")\n\nclass VendorPricing(BaseModel):\n    vendor: str = Field(description=\"Company name\")\n    tiers: List[PricingTier] = Field(description=\"Available pricing tiers\")\n```\n\n```python\ndoc = app.scrape(\n    \"https://resend.com/pricing\",\n    formats=[\n        \"markdown\",\n        {\"type\": \"json\", \"schema\": VendorPricing.model_json_schema()},\n    ],\n)\n\npricing = VendorPricing(**doc.json)\nprint(f\"{pricing.vendor} Pricing:\")\nfor tier in pricing.tiers:\n    print(f\"  {tier.name}: {tier.price} ({tier.email_limit})\")\n```\n\n```\nResend Pricing:\n  Free: $0/mo (3,000 emails/mo)\n  Pro: $20/mo (50,000 emails/mo)\n  Scale: $90/mo (100,000 emails/mo)\n  Enterprise: Custom (Custom volume)\n```\n\nThe `formats` list takes strings and dicts together. `\"markdown\"` gets the raw page text, and the JSON format dict tells the API to run LLM extraction against your schema. Both come back in the same response.\n\n`\"json\"` as a bare string in the formats list won't work. The JSON format requires the dict form with `type` and either `schema` or `prompt`.\n\n`Field(description=...)` is worth getting right. Those descriptions ship to the API as part of the JSON schema, and the extraction model reads them to decide what qualifies as a match. Something vague like `\"the value\"` won't give the model much to work with.\n\n`doc.json` comes back as a plain dict, not a Pydantic instance. The `VendorPricing(**doc.json)` call validates the structure. In production, wrap it in a try/except to catch cases where extraction doesn't match your schema.\n\nCost: 1 credit base plus 4 for JSON extraction, so 5 credits per page.\n\n### Multi-page crawling with /crawl\n\nSome features need content from an entire section of a site, not just one page. The [/crawl endpoint](https://docs.firecrawl.dev/features/crawl) starts at a URL, follows links within that path, and scrapes each page it finds.\n\n```python\nresult = app.crawl(\n    \"https://resend.com/docs\",\n    limit=10,\n    include_paths=[\"/docs/*\"],\n    scrape_options=ScrapeOptions(formats=[\"markdown\"]),\n)\n\nprint(f\"Crawled {result.completed} pages ({result.credits_used} credits)\")\nprint()\nfor doc in result.data:\n    print(f\"  {doc.metadata.title}: {len(doc.markdown)} chars\")\n```\n\n```\nCrawled 10 pages (10 credits)\n\n  Introduction - Resend: 3,842 chars\n  Send with SMTP - Resend: 2,156 chars\n  Send with Next.js - Resend: 4,521 chars\n  Webhooks - Resend: 1,893 chars\n  ...\n```\n\n`include_paths` takes glob patterns to restrict which URLs get crawled. Without it, the crawler follows every link on the domain and you'll end up scraping unrelated sections of the site.\n\nSet `limit` deliberately. The API default is 10,000 pages, and at 1 credit per page that adds up fast. For most use cases, 10 to 20 pages gives you enough content to work with.\n\nSmall crawls of 3 to 5 pages finish in under 10 seconds. Larger ones can take minutes. The blocking `.crawl()` method polls the server automatically and returns once the job completes.\n\nEach page comes back as a `Document`, the same type scrape returns. You could concatenate the markdown and pass it to an LLM for analysis, or index each page in a [vector store](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/best-vector-databases) for retrieval later.\n\nCost: 1 credit per page crawled.\n\n### Prompt-driven research with /agent\n\nThe previous three endpoints require you to know what to search for or which URLs to hit. The [/agent endpoint](https://docs.firecrawl.dev/features/agent) works differently: you write a prompt describing what you need, and it finds relevant pages, reads them, and returns structured data. You don't need URLs.\n\nThe `/agent` endpoint is the right pattern for questions where the answer is spread across multiple sources and you can't predict which pages will have it.\n\n```python\nclass VendorAssessment(BaseModel):\n    vendor: str = Field(description=\"Company name\")\n    strengths: List[str] = Field(description=\"Top 3 strengths based on developer feedback\")\n    weaknesses: List[str] = Field(description=\"Top 3 weaknesses or common complaints\")\n    best_for: str = Field(description=\"What type of project or team this vendor fits best\")\n```\n\n```python\nresult = app.agent(\n    prompt=\"\"\"Research Resend as a transactional email API provider.\nLook at developer community feedback, GitHub issues,\nand reviews. What are the main strengths, weaknesses,\nand ideal use case?\"\"\",\n    schema=VendorAssessment,\n    model=\"spark-1-mini\",\n)\n\nassessment = VendorAssessment(**result.data)\nprint(f\"{assessment.vendor}:\")\nprint(f\"  Best for: {assessment.best_for}\")\nprint(f\"  Strengths:\")\nfor s in assessment.strengths:\n    print(f\"    - {s}\")\nprint(f\"  Weaknesses:\")\nfor w in assessment.weaknesses:\n    print(f\"    - {w}\")\n```\n\nWait a moment for the agent to run.\n\n```\nResend:\n  Best for: Developers and startups wanting a modern, developer-first email API\n  Strengths:\n    - Clean, well-documented API with intuitive SDK design\n    - Native React Email integration for building templates in JSX\n    - Simple pricing with generous free tier\n  Weaknesses:\n    - Younger platform with less enterprise track record than SendGrid\n    - Fewer integrations and add-ons compared to established providers\n    - Limited analytics and reporting features\n```\n\nUnlike scrape's JSON format, the agent endpoint accepts a Pydantic class directly as the `schema` argument. No `.model_json_schema()` call needed.\n\nTwo [model choices](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/introducing-spark-1): `spark-1-mini` (default) costs less and works well for straightforward questions. `spark-1-pro` is better for complex or ambiguous research. Credit usage varies with how many pages the agent visits and how much reasoning it does, so there's no fixed per-call cost like with scrape.\n\nAgent calls take 30 to 60 seconds. The agent browses the web, loads pages, and reasons about the content before responding. Build appropriate loading states into your UX.\n\nThe prompt matters more here than with the other endpoints. Be specific about what sources you want checked and what kind of information you're after. \"Research Resend\" is vaguer than pointing it toward community feedback and real-world usage patterns.\n\n| Endpoint  | Pattern                                | Input                            | Cost                                  |\n| --------- | -------------------------------------- | -------------------------------- | ------------------------------------- |\n| `/search` | Find pages + get content               | Search query                     | 2 credits/10 results + 1/page scraped |\n| `/scrape` | Structured extraction from a URL       | URL + Pydantic schema            | 1 credit (5 with JSON extraction)     |\n| `/crawl`  | Multi-page content from a site section | Start URL + path filters         | 1 credit/page                         |\n| `/agent`  | Prompt-driven research, no URLs needed | Natural language prompt + schema | Variable per call                     |\n\n![Decision tree for choosing between Firecrawl search scrape crawl and agent endpoints for AI-powered data retrieval](/images/blog/ai-powered-data-retrieval/endpoint_decision_tree.webp)\n\nIn practice, most features won't need all four endpoints. A search widget might only use `/search`, while a data import tool could chain it with `/scrape`. The vendor evaluation example ties them together to show how they compose, but each one stands on its own.\n\n## For AI agent developers\n\nThe code in this tutorial runs as a standalone script, but the same endpoints work as tools inside [agent frameworks](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/best-open-source-agent-frameworks). Your agent decides when to search, scrape, or crawl, and calls Firecrawl through whichever integration fits your stack.\n\n[LangChain](https://python.langchain.com)'s `FireCrawlLoader` in `langchain-community` wraps scraping and crawling as a document loader. LlamaIndex has `FireCrawlWebReader` for the same purpose. CrewAI ships three separate tool classes covering scrape, crawl, and search. If you're using a custom agent loop, the Python SDK (`firecrawl-py`) and Node SDK (`@mendable/firecrawl-js`) work as direct function calls without any framework glue.\n\nThe fastest integration path for [MCP](https://modelcontextprotocol.io)-compatible platforms is Firecrawl's [MCP server](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/best-mcp-servers-for-developers). Run `npx firecrawl-mcp` with your API key, and you get 12 tools exposed through the Model Context Protocol: the four endpoints from this tutorial, plus map, batch scrape, browser automation, and async job management. There's also a hosted MCP endpoint if you'd rather skip running the server locally.\n\nThe `/agent` endpoint is already an agent itself. Calling it from your agent creates two layers, where yours runs orchestration and business logic while Firecrawl's runs the web research. While this split can make sense for deep research tasks, managing browser sessions and page parsing inside your own agent loop adds a bunch of complexity. Firecrawl's agent abstracts that away.\n\nFor agents that need to interact with pages beyond just reading them (filling forms, clicking through pagination, handling login flows), Firecrawl's [browser sandbox](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/introducing-browser-sandbox) provides managed cloud sessions with Playwright and CDP access. And when your agent needs to run the same research across hundreds of entities at once, [Parallel Agents](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/introducing-parallel-agents) lets you submit batch `/agent` jobs that run concurrently instead of looping through them one by one.\n\n## Best practices for production\n\nIf you don't have URLs yet, start with `/search`. For known URLs, `/scrape` takes a single page and `/crawl` takes multiple pages on the same domain. Reach for `/agent` when the question is open-ended enough that you can't specify sources upfront. Agent calls cost more and run slower, so defaulting to the simpler endpoints keeps your feature fast and your bill predictable.\n\nThe scrape endpoint accepts a `max_age` parameter (in milliseconds) that returns cached content when it's fresh enough. For pages that don't change every hour, you can avoid redundant scrapes and save credits.\n\n```python\ndoc = app.scrape(\"https://resend.com/pricing\", max_age=14400000)  # 4 hours\n```\n\nRate limits depend on your plan. The API returns HTTP 429 when you hit them. The SDK manages retries through `max_retries` and `backoff_factor` on initialization:\n\n```python\napp = Firecrawl(api_key=\"...\", max_retries=3, backoff_factor=0.5)\n```\n\nKeep schemas flat when you can. Nested Pydantic models work, but flatter schemas produce more accurate extractions because the LLM has less structural ambiguity to resolve. Use `Optional` fields for data that might not exist on every page.\n\n## Conclusion\nWhich endpoints you need depends on your feature. A URL import button might only use `/scrape`. A research assistant could chain search, crawl, and agent calls. The retrieval pattern stays the same regardless: you define a schema for the data you want, and the API does the fetching and formatting. Whether it searches the web or scrapes a URL you provide, the output comes back typed and ready to store.\n\nThe [documentation](https://docs.firecrawl.dev) covers options beyond what this tutorial included, and both the Python and [Node](https://docs.firecrawl.dev/sdks/node) SDKs support every endpoint shown here."])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"9e:T5c98,"])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"\n## TL;DR\n- Gemini Live handles audio natively in the model, so the stream stays open even during tool calls like web search\n- We build a voice assistant with three tools: [Firecrawl search](https://docs.firecrawl.dev/api-reference/endpoint/search) for live web data, Gmail read, and Gmail send\n- LiveKit Agents handles WebRTC transport and deployment to the cloud\n- The entire agent fits in a single `agent.py` file with about 150 lines of Python\n\n---\n\nMost voice assistant pipelines force a rigid loop. They transcribe speech to text, send it to an LLM, and convert the response back to audio. Adding tool calls like web search makes this worse because the connection stalls while waiting for results, and the conversation feels like talking to a hold queue.\n\n[Gemini Live](https://ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/live) takes a different approach by handling audio natively in the model itself, so the stream stays open even when the model calls external tools mid-conversation. \n\nWe'll use this to build a voice assistant that searches the web through [Firecrawl](https://firecrawl.dev) and manages your Gmail inbox, all in spoken conversation. The stack is Gemini Live API, [LiveKit Agents](https://docs.livekit.io/agents/) for WebRTC transport, Firecrawl for search, and Gmail SMTP/IMAP for email, packed into a single `agent.py` deployed to LiveKit Cloud.\n\n![Traditional voice pipeline with STT LLM TTS steps that stall on tool calls vs Gemini Live native audio handling audio in tool calls and audio out in a single unbroken stream](/images/blog/real-time-voice-assistant-gemini-firecrawl/traditional-vs-native-audio-pipeline.webp)\n\n## Setup and Prerequisites\nYou need Python 3.10+ and accounts on four services (all with free tiers):\n\n- [Google AI Studio](https://ai.google.dev/) for a Gemini API key\n- [LiveKit Cloud](https://cloud.livekit.io/) for WebRTC transport\n- [Firecrawl](https://www.firecrawl.dev/pricing) for the web search API (free tier available)\n- A Gmail account with 2-Step Verification for email tools (Optional)\n\nCreate your project directory and install dependencies:\n\n```bash\nmkdir voice-assistant \u0026\u0026 cd voice-assistant\nuv init\nuv add \"livekit-agents[google]\u003e=1.4\" \"firecrawl-py\u003e=1.0\" \"python-dotenv\u003e=1.0\" \"imap-tools\u003e=1.0\"\n```\n\nThe `livekit-agents[google]` package includes LiveKit's Gemini plugin. Version 1.4 or higher is required because earlier versions don't support the latest native audio models.\n\nGrab your API keys from each service. For Firecrawl, sign up at [firecrawl.dev](https://firecrawl.dev) and copy your API key from the dashboard:\n\n![Firecrawl developer dashboard with API key for voice assistant web search integration](/images/blog/real-time-voice-assistant-gemini-firecrawl/firecrawl-dashboard-api-keys.webp)\n\nFor LiveKit, create a new Cloud project and note the URL, API key, and secret:\n\n![LiveKit Cloud project dashboard with API key and secret for WebRTC voice agent deployment](/images/blog/real-time-voice-assistant-gemini-firecrawl/livekit-dashboard-api-keys.webp)\n\nIf you want to use the email feature, consider creating a new test gmail account for this tutorial. To connect, Gmail needs an App Password instead of your regular password. Go to your Google Account security settings and turn on 2-Step Verification first:\n\n![Google Account security settings with 2-Step Verification enabled for Gmail App Password setup](/images/blog/real-time-voice-assistant-gemini-firecrawl/google-2step-verification.webp)\n\nThen visit [myaccount.google.com/apppasswords](https://myaccount.google.com/apppasswords), name your app password, and save the generated 16-character code:\n\n![Gmail App Password generation page showing the 16 character password for SMTP and IMAP voice assistant email access](/images/blog/real-time-voice-assistant-gemini-firecrawl/gmail-app-password-generated.webp)\n\nThen, create a `.env` file with all your credentials:\n\n```\nGOOGLE_API_KEY=your_google_api_key\nFIRECRAWL_API_KEY=your_firecrawl_api_key\nLIVEKIT_URL=wss://your-project.livekit.cloud\nLIVEKIT_API_KEY=your_livekit_api_key\nLIVEKIT_API_SECRET=your_livekit_api_secret\nGMAIL_ADDRESS=your_email@gmail.com\nGMAIL_APP_PASSWORD=your_app_password\n```\n\nNote: If you run into issues with the email read tool, confirm IMAP access is on in Gmail (although this should be active by default). Go to Settings \u003e Forwarding and POP/IMAP and turn on IMAP access if it's off. Without this, the email read tool won't connect.\n\n## The Voice Agent Core\nCreate `agent.py` in your project directory. [Here's the complete file as a GitHub Gist](https://gist.github.com/BexTuychiev/9d5fbb22d58db26f1147c60ff48978a7) so you can reference the full picture in a separate tab while we break it down piece by piece.\n\n- [The voice conversation core](#the-voice-agent-core) (this section)\n- [Web search](#adding-live-web-search-with-firecrawl) (next section)\n- [Email tools](#adding-email-with-gmail) (the section after)\n\n![Voice assistant system architecture showing user browser connected via WebRTC to LiveKit Agents which streams native audio to Gemini Live with Firecrawl search and Gmail as tool integrations](/images/blog/real-time-voice-assistant-gemini-firecrawl/voice-assistant-system-architecture.webp)\n\nStart with the imports and setup:\n\n```python\nimport asyncio\nimport os\n\nfrom livekit.agents import (\n    Agent,\n    AgentSession,\n    AgentServer,\n    JobContext,\n    cli,\n    function_tool,\n)\nfrom livekit.plugins import google\nfrom dotenv import load_dotenv\n\nload_dotenv()\n```\n\nThree classes from `livekit.agents` do the heavy lifting. `AgentServer` handles incoming WebRTC connections from LiveKit Cloud. `AgentSession` manages a single live conversation. `Agent` defines the assistant's behavior. We'll also need `function_tool` later to expose Python functions as callable tools for the model.\n\nNext, define the agent by subclassing `Agent`:\n\n```python\nclass ResearchAssistant(Agent):\n    def __init__(self):\n        super().__init__(\n            instructions=\"You are a helpful assistant. \"\n            \"Keep responses conversational and concise. \"\n            \"Speak naturally, as if having a conversation.\",\n        )\n```\n\nThe `instructions` parameter is the system prompt. We'll expand it later when we add tools, but a simple prompt is enough to test the voice connection.\n\nNow the `entrypoint` that ties everything together:\n\n```python\nserver = AgentServer()\n\n\n@server.rtc_session()\nasync def entrypoint(ctx: JobContext):\n    await ctx.connect()\n\n    session = AgentSession(\n        llm=google.realtime.RealtimeModel(\n            model=\"gemini-2.5-flash-native-audio-preview-12-2025\",\n            voice=\"Puck\",\n        ),\n    )\n\n    await session.start(\n        room=ctx.room,\n        agent=ResearchAssistant(),\n    )\n\n\nif __name__ == \"__main__\":\n    cli.run_app(server)\n```\n\nThe `@server.rtc_session()` decorator registers `entrypoint` as the handler for each new voice session. When someone connects, LiveKit calls this function with a `JobContext` representing the room.\n\n`RealtimeModel` configures the Gemini connection. The model name `gemini-2.5-flash-native-audio-preview-12-2025` is worth paying attention to because it uses native audio mode, meaning audio goes directly into and out of the model without intermediate speech-to-text or text-to-speech steps. \n\nThis gives lower latency and more natural responses compared to the cascaded approach, where a separate STT model transcribes your voice, an LLM generates text, and a TTS model reads it back. The `voice` parameter picks from Gemini's built-in voices. \"Puck\" is a solid default, though you can also try \"Charon\", \"Kore\", or \"Aoede\".\n\nTest it:\n\n```bash\nuv run python agent.py dev\n```\n\nThis starts the agent in development mode and connects it to your LiveKit Cloud project. Open the [LiveKit Agent Playground](https://agents-playground.livekit.io/) and select your project:\n\n![LiveKit Agent Playground project selector ready to connect to the Gemini Live voice assistant](/images/blog/real-time-voice-assistant-gemini-firecrawl/livekit-sandbox-voice-agent.webp)\n\nOnce connected, click \"start call\" from the web UI. The first connection takes a moment, but as soon as you see \"Started reading stream\" in the logs, you're good to go. All responses should be sub 1-second if your network connection is solid.\n\n![Active real-time voice conversation with the Gemini Live assistant in the LiveKit Agent Playground](/images/blog/real-time-voice-assistant-gemini-firecrawl/livekit-playground-active-conversation.webp)\n\nAt this point, the assistant can only chat using Gemini's built-in knowledge, with no access to current information or external tools.\n\n## Adding Live Web Search with Firecrawl\nThe assistant needs access to live web data to be useful beyond general conversation. [Firecrawl's search endpoint](https://docs.firecrawl.dev/api-reference/endpoint/search) combines web search and content extraction in a single API call, which is exactly what a voice agent needs. For a deeper look at the search endpoint's options, there's a [full walkthrough on the Firecrawl blog](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/mastering-firecrawl-search-endpoint).\n\nAdd the Firecrawl import to the top of `agent.py`:\n\n```python\nfrom firecrawl import Firecrawl\n```\n\nThen create the search tool:\n\n```python\n@function_tool\nasync def web_search(query: str) -\u003e str:\n    \"\"\"Search the web for current information on any topic.\n\n    Args:\n        query: The search query to look up\n\n    Returns:\n        Search results with titles and descriptions\n    \"\"\"\n    firecrawl = Firecrawl(\n        api_key=os.getenv(\"FIRECRAWL_API_KEY\")\n    )\n\n    try:\n        results = firecrawl.search(query=query, limit=5)\n    except Exception as e:\n        return f\"Search failed: {str(e)}\"\n```\n\nThe `@function_tool` decorator from LiveKit exposes this function to Gemini as a callable tool. The docstring and type hints are sent to the model as the tool's schema, which is how Gemini knows when and how to call the function during a conversation.\n\n`Firecrawl.search()` sends the query to Firecrawl's API through the [Python SDK](https://docs.firecrawl.dev/sdks/python) and returns structured results. The `limit=5` caps the number of results so the model doesn't get overwhelmed with data to read aloud.\n\nThe SDK (v2) returns results as Pydantic models, with web results living on the `.web` attribute. Since the response structure can vary across SDK versions, we add fallbacks:\n\n```python\n    web_results = (\n        getattr(results, \"web\", None)\n        or getattr(results, \"data\", None)\n        or []\n    )\n\n    if not web_results:\n        return \"No results found for that query.\"\n```\n\nThen format each result. The `isinstance` check handles both Pydantic model and dict responses for compatibility:\n\n```python\n    formatted = []\n    for item in web_results:\n        title = (\n            getattr(item, \"title\", \"Untitled\")\n            if not isinstance(item, dict)\n            else item.get(\"title\", \"Untitled\")\n        )\n        description = (\n            getattr(item, \"description\", \"\")\n            if not isinstance(item, dict)\n            else item.get(\"description\", \"\")\n        )\n        url = (\n            getattr(item, \"url\", \"\")\n            if not isinstance(item, dict)\n            else item.get(\"url\", \"\")\n        )\n        formatted.append(\n            f\"- {title}: {description} (Source: {url})\"\n        )\n\n    return \"\\n\".join(formatted)\n```\n\nThe model reads these results conversationally, pulling out what's relevant to your question and citing the sources.\n\nRegister the tool in `AgentSession`:\n\n```python\nsession = AgentSession(\n    llm=google.realtime.RealtimeModel(\n        model=\"gemini-2.5-flash-native-audio-preview-12-2025\",\n        voice=\"Puck\",\n    ),\n    tools=[web_search],\n)\n```\n\nWhat makes the Gemini Live integration stand out here is how tool calls work in the audio stream. When you ask something like \"What's the weather in Tokyo?\", the model calls `web_search` while keeping the audio connection open. You might hear a brief pause, but the conversation doesn't disconnect. The model receives the search results and speaks the answer without re-establishing anything. \n\nThis full-duplex behavior is the main advantage over traditional voice pipelines that stop listening, process, and start speaking again as separate steps. If you want to see how a similar pattern works with OpenAI's realtime API, Firecrawl's team wrote a [tutorial on building conversational agents that talk with any website](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/How-to-Talk-with-Any-Website-Using-OpenAIs-Realtime-API-and-Firecrawl).\n\n![Gemini Live tool call flow showing the audio stream staying open while the user speaks then Gemini calls Firecrawl web search then results return and Gemini speaks the answer](/images/blog/real-time-voice-assistant-gemini-firecrawl/gemini-live-tool-call-flow.webp)\n\nRestart the agent with `uv run python agent.py dev` and try asking about something current through the playground.\n\n## Adding Email with Gmail\nWeb search covers information retrieval. Email adds something more practical— the assistant can read your inbox aloud and send messages on your behalf, just by you asking.\n\n---\n\n***⚠ Security Warning: Prompt Injection Risk***\n\n*Agents with access to external data sources are vulnerable to **prompt injection** — where malicious content embedded in a webpage or email manipulates the agent into taking unintended actions. This agent is exposed to both web content (via Firecrawl) and raw email data, and it has the power to send emails on your behalf.*\n\n*A hijacked agent could be tricked into forwarding sensitive emails to an attacker or sending crafted messages without your knowledge. The risk is low in a personal, single-user setup like this tutorial, but it is real.*\n\n***Mitigations to consider:** use a dedicated test Gmail account (not your primary inbox), avoid granting access to emails containing passwords or financial data, add an explicit confirmation step before any email is sent, and never expose this agent publicly without proper sandboxing and output filtering.*\n\n---\n\nGmail's full API requires OAuth with a Google Cloud Console project, which would add a lot of setup to this tutorial. The simpler path: SMTP for sending and IMAP for reading, authenticated with the App Password from the setup section.\n\nAdd the remaining imports to the top of `agent.py`:\n\n```python\nimport smtplib\nfrom email.mime.text import MIMEText\nfrom imap_tools import MailBox\n```\n\nHere's the first part of the read tool:\n\n```python\n@function_tool\nasync def read_emails(count: int = 5) -\u003e str:\n    \"\"\"Read recent emails from the inbox.\n\n    Args:\n        count: Number of recent emails to fetch (default 5, max 10)\n\n    Returns:\n        A summary of recent emails with sender, subject, and preview\n    \"\"\"\n    count = min(count, 10)\n    gmail_user = os.getenv(\"GMAIL_ADDRESS\")\n    gmail_password = os.getenv(\"GMAIL_APP_PASSWORD\")\n```\n\nThe `count` parameter has a hard cap at 10 to keep responses manageable for voice output. Gemini sees the docstring and can tell users about the default and limits.\n\nBoth `imap_tools` and `smtplib` are synchronous libraries. To avoid blocking the async event loop, every blocking call gets wrapped in `asyncio.to_thread()`:\n\n```python\n    def _fetch():\n        with MailBox(\"imap.gmail.com\").login(\n            gmail_user, gmail_password\n        ) as mailbox:\n            emails = []\n            for msg in mailbox.fetch(\n                limit=count, reverse=True\n            ):\n                body_preview = (\n                    (msg.text or \"\")[:150]\n                    .replace(\"\\n\", \" \").strip()\n                )\n                emails.append(\n                    f\"- From: {msg.from_}\\n\"\n                    f\"  Subject: {msg.subject}\\n\"\n                    f\"  Preview: {body_preview}\"\n                )\n            return emails\n\n    try:\n        emails = await asyncio.to_thread(_fetch)\n    except Exception as e:\n        return f\"Failed to read emails: {str(e)}\"\n\n    if not emails:\n        return \"No emails found in inbox.\"\n\n    return (\n        f\"Here are your {len(emails)} most recent emails:\\n\\n\"\n        + \"\\n\\n\".join(emails)\n    )\n```\n\n`MailBox(\"imap.gmail.com\")` connects to Gmail's IMAP server. The `reverse=True` flag fetches newest messages first. Body previews are truncated to 150 characters since long email bodies don't translate well to spoken responses.\n\nThe send tool follows the same `asyncio.to_thread()` pattern:\n\n```python\n@function_tool\nasync def send_email(to: str, subject: str, body: str) -\u003e str:\n    \"\"\"Send an email to someone.\n\n    Args:\n        to: Recipient email address\n        subject: Email subject line\n        body: Email body text\n\n    Returns:\n        Confirmation that the email was sent\n    \"\"\"\n    gmail_user = os.getenv(\"GMAIL_ADDRESS\")\n    gmail_password = os.getenv(\"GMAIL_APP_PASSWORD\")\n\n    msg = MIMEText(body)\n    msg[\"Subject\"] = subject\n    msg[\"From\"] = gmail_user\n    msg[\"To\"] = to\n```\n\n```python\n    def _send():\n        with smtplib.SMTP_SSL(\"smtp.gmail.com\", 465) as server:\n            server.login(gmail_user, gmail_password)\n            server.send_message(msg)\n\n    try:\n        await asyncio.to_thread(_send)\n    except Exception as e:\n        return f\"Failed to send email: {str(e)}\"\n\n    return f\"Email sent to {to} with subject '{subject}'.\"\n```\n\n`SMTP_SSL` connects directly over TLS on port 465. Same wrapping pattern as the read tool.\n\nNow register all three tools and update the agent's system instructions to handle email behavior:\n\n```python\nsession = AgentSession(\n    llm=google.realtime.RealtimeModel(\n        model=\"gemini-2.5-flash-native-audio-preview-12-2025\",\n        voice=\"Puck\",\n    ),\n    tools=[web_search, read_emails, send_email],\n)\n```\n\nReplace the minimal `ResearchAssistant` class with the full version that covers all three tools:\n\n```python\nclass ResearchAssistant(Agent):\n    def __init__(self):\n        super().__init__(\n            instructions=\"\"\"You are a helpful research assistant with access to web search and email.\n\nYour role:\n- Help users find information on any topic using web search\n- Read and summarize their recent emails when asked\n- Send emails on their behalf when they provide a recipient, subject, and message\n- Keep responses conversational and concise since this is a voice interaction\n- When you use search results, mention where the information came from\n- If you don't know something and search doesn't help, say so honestly\n\nFor email:\n- Before sending an email, always confirm the recipient, subject, and message with the user\n- When reading emails, give a brief spoken summary rather than reading every detail\n- Never send an email without the user explicitly asking you to\n\nStyle guidelines:\n- Speak naturally, as if having a conversation\n- Avoid long lists or complex formatting that doesn't work well in speech\n- Break up information into digestible pieces\n- Ask clarifying questions if a request is ambiguous\"\"\",\n        )\n```\n\nThe confirmation step before sending is the most important instruction here. You don't want the model firing off emails based on a misheard word.\n\nOne thing to note: `imap_tools` reads individual messages, not Gmail threads. If a back-and-forth email chain has five messages, you'll see each one separately rather than as a grouped conversation. Full thread support would require the Gmail API with OAuth, which is a natural next step if you want to extend this project.\n\nRestart the agent with `uv run python agent.py dev` and test all three tools through the playground. Try asking it to search for something, read your recent emails, and send a test email.\n\n## Production Deployment\nThe agent works in development mode on your machine, but LiveKit Cloud can host it so it's always available without keeping a terminal open.\n\nStart with a `Dockerfile`. LiveKit recommends using [uv](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/) for faster dependency installation:\n\n```dockerfile\nFROM ghcr.io/astral-sh/uv:python3.12-bookworm-slim\n\nENV PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1\n\nARG UID=10001\nRUN adduser \\\n    --disabled-password \\\n    --gecos \"\" \\\n    --home \"/app\" \\\n    --shell \"/sbin/nologin\" \\\n    --uid \"${UID}\" \\\n    appuser\n\nRUN apt-get update \u0026\u0026 apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \\\n    gcc python3-dev \\\n    \u0026\u0026 rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*\n\nWORKDIR /app\n\nCOPY requirements.txt .\nRUN uv pip install --system --no-cache -r requirements.txt\n\nCOPY . .\n\nRUN chown -R appuser:appuser /app\nUSER appuser\n\nCMD [\"python\", \"agent.py\", \"start\"]\n```\n\nThe `uv` base image includes Python 3.12 and the uv package manager. `uv pip install --system` installs dependencies into the container's system Python, which is faster than pip and doesn't need a virtual environment inside a container. The non-privileged `appuser` follows LiveKit Cloud's security requirements. Requirements are copied before the application code so dependency installation gets cached across builds.\n\nNext, create `livekit.toml`:\n\n```toml\n[project]\nname = \"gemini-voice-assistant\"\n\n[agent]\nentrypoint = \"agent.py\"\n```\n\nThe CLI reads this file to identify your project and entrypoint during deployment.\n\nInstall the LiveKit CLI:\n\n```bash\n# macOS\nbrew install livekit-cli\n\n# Linux\ncurl -sSL https://get.livekit.io/cli | bash\n```\n\nAuthenticate with your LiveKit Cloud account:\n\n```bash\nlk cloud auth\n```\n\nThis opens a browser window to link the CLI to your project. Once authenticated, deploy the agent:\n\n```bash\nlk agent init\n```\n\nThis command registers your agent, uploads your code, builds the container from your Dockerfile, and deploys it to LiveKit Cloud. The CLI detects your `.env` file and prompts you to upload the values as encrypted secrets that get injected as environment variables at runtime. Note that `LIVEKIT_URL`, `LIVEKIT_API_KEY`, and `LIVEKIT_API_SECRET` are auto-provided by LiveKit Cloud, so you can skip those during the secrets prompt.\n\nOnce deployed, open the [Agent Playground](https://agents-playground.livekit.io/), select your project, and start a call to test the cloud-hosted agent:\n\n![Gemini Live voice assistant with Firecrawl web search deployed to LiveKit Cloud and running in the Agent Playground](/images/blog/real-time-voice-assistant-gemini-firecrawl/livekit-cloud-deployed-agent-conversation.webp)\n\nFor subsequent code changes:\n\n```bash\nlk agent deploy\n```\n\nThis builds and deploys a new version using a rolling strategy: new sessions route to the updated agent while active sessions finish on the old one.\n\nMonitor your deployed agent with:\n\n```bash\nlk agent status   # Replica count and health\nlk agent logs     # Tail live logs\n```\n\nAnd if a deployment goes wrong:\n\n```bash\nlk agent rollback\n```\n\nThis reverts to the previous version without rebuilding.\n\n## Conclusion\nWe started with an empty directory and built a voice assistant that carries on a natural conversation, searches the web through [Firecrawl's search API](https://docs.firecrawl.dev/api-reference/endpoint/search), and reads and sends email through Gmail. The full-duplex nature of Gemini Live means tool calls happen without breaking the audio stream, and LiveKit Cloud handles the deployment and scaling.\n\nA few directions to take this further:\n\n- Swap `imap_tools` for the Gmail API to get full thread support\n- Add more tools like calendar access, Slack messaging, or database queries\n- Try different Gemini voices (\"Charon\", \"Kore\", \"Aoede\") through the `voice` parameter\n- Build a custom frontend with [LiveKit's React SDK](https://docs.livekit.io/frontends/) instead of using the playground\n- Add a [scrape tool](https://docs.firecrawl.dev/api-reference/endpoint/scrape) alongside search so the agent can read full pages when search snippets aren't enough"])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"9f:T5e0d,"])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"\nMost AI assistants live in a browser tab. They reset between sessions and can't do anything on your machine without you copy-pasting back and forth.\n\n[Peter Steinberger](https://github.com/steipete) wanted something different. He built an AI agent that runs on your own hardware and remembers you across sessions. You talk to it through Telegram while it works on your desktop.\n\nWhat started as a weekend hack became the fastest-growing repository in GitHub history: 175,000+ stars in under three months, growing 18x faster than Kubernetes. It was called ClawdBot, then MoltBot (Anthropic's trademark lawyers had opinions), and now OpenClaw (as of Feb 16, 2025, OpenClaw has officially joined hands with OpenAI).\n\nI've been running OpenClaw for weeks now, and I'm sharing what I learned. This guide covers what OpenClaw is, how to get started, what the community is building with it, and how to give your agent access to live web data through Firecrawl.\n\n## TL;DR\n\n- OpenClaw (formerly ClawdBot, then MoltBot) is a self-hosted AI agent you control from your phone through Telegram, WhatsApp, or other messaging apps\n- It runs a local gateway on your hardware and uses LLMs to execute tasks on your machine\n- This article covers OpenClaw's origins, what the community is building, personal lessons from daily use, and adding Firecrawl for live web data access\n\n## What is OpenClaw, MoltBot, ClawdBot?\nOpenClaw is a self-hosted AI agent you control from your phone through Telegram, WhatsApp, or other messaging apps. It runs on your own hardware with full system access (browser, terminal, filesystem) and remembers you across sessions.\n\nOpenClaw's creator is Peter Steinberger, an Austrian iOS developer. He previously built the company PSPDFKit, a PDF SDK used on over a billion devices, that he sold in 2021 for roughly 100 million euros. He then went through what he described as \"profound existential emptiness\", and his GitHub activity went dark for three years.\n\nSteinberger came back to coding in April 2025 and started experimenting with AI. By November, he had a working prototype: a WebSocket gateway that relayed WhatsApp messages to Claude's CLI agent. The agent ran shell commands on his machine and sent results back through chat. He named it ClawdBot (a lobster-claw spin on \"Claude\") and open-sourced it.\n\nThe project hit 9,000 stars on its first day. TechCrunch and Hacker News picked it up, and within a week it had more Google searches than Claude Code and Codex combined.\n\nThen Anthropic's trademark team sent a letter (\"Clawd\" sounded too much like \"Claude\".) Steinberger renamed it to MoltBot on January 27, 2026, a reference to molting lobsters do to grow. Three days later, he renamed it again to OpenClaw because MoltBot \"never quite rolled off the tongue.\" Reddit called it \"the fastest triple rebrand in open source history.\"\n\nNow, OpenAI has joined hands with OpenClaw.\n\n\u003cdiv style={{ maxWidth: 550, margin: \"0 auto\" }}\u003e\n  \u003ciframe\n    src=\"https://platform.twitter.com/embed/Tweet.html?id=2023150230905159801\u0026lang=en\u0026theme=light\u0026dnt=true\"\n    style={{ width: \"100%\", height: \"clamp(420px, 70vh, 200px)\", border: 0 }}\n    loading=\"lazy\"\n    title=\"Embedded Tweet\"\n  /\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\n### How it works\n\nOpenClaw has two components: a gateway server and an agent runtime. The gateway runs on your machine (Mac Mini, Linux box, even WSL2 on Windows) and connects to Telegram, WhatsApp, Slack, Discord, Signal, iMessage, and a handful of others.\n\nWhen you send a message, the gateway passes it to the agent, which calls an LLM and executes whatever the model decides to do. It supports Claude, GPT, DeepSeek, Gemini, and local models through [Ollama](https://ollama.com).\n\n![OpenClaw architecture showing message flow from phone through gateway server and agent runtime to LLM provider with system access to terminal, browser, and filesystem](/images/blog/openclaw-firecrawl-guide/openclaw-message-architecture.webp)\n\nOpenClaw runs on your hardware, not in a browser tab. It has full system access: browser automation, email, terminal, filesystem. It also remembers you across sessions through workspace files:\n\n- **SOUL.md** defines its personality\n- **USER.md** stores your preferences\n- **AGENTS.md** holds its instructions\n\nYou talk to it from your phone while it works on another machine.\n\n### Quick note on security\n\nOpenClaw runs with full access to your machine's terminal, filesystem, and browser. Running it on your primary computer carries real risk. The easiest safe setup for most users is a cloud VM or another cheap, isolated machine you can wipe if anything goes wrong. A DigitalOcean Droplet ($6/month) or Google Cloud e2-micro (free tier eligible) both work well, although the current recommendation is a Mac Mini. I ended up running OpenClaw on EC2. I'll go into more about this later on.\n\n### Getting started\n\nSetup takes about ten minutes. On your chosen VM, install OpenClaw and run the onboarding wizard:\n\n```bash\nnpm install -g openclaw@latest\nopenclaw onboard --install-daemon\n```\n\nAlternatively, the docs recommend curl to install:\n\n```\ncurl -fsSL https://openclaw.ai/install.sh | bash\n```\n\nThe wizard walks you through picking a model provider (Claude via Anthropic API or a Pro/Max subscription, GPT, DeepSeek, or a local model through Ollama) and basic security settings.\n\nOnce onboarding completes, verify your gateway is actually running before moving on: `openclaw gateway status`. If it's not, run `openclaw gateway start` and check for errors.\n\nIf anything looks off, `openclaw doctor` runs a diagnostic check on your gateway, channels, and model provider configuration. For a full walkthrough with screenshots, check the [official docs](https://docs.openclaw.ai/start/getting-started).\n\nOnce onboarding finishes, connect Telegram. Open [@BotFather](https://core.telegram.org/bots#botfather) in Telegram, send `/newbot`, and follow the prompts to get a bot token. Then:\n\n```bash\nopenclaw channels login --channel telegram\n```\n\nPaste your bot token when prompted. The gateway connects to Telegram and your bot goes online. Send it a message to verify everything works. Something like \"What time is it?\" is enough. If the agent responds in chat, you're set.\n\nIf any issues arise, use the `openclaw doctor`.\n\n## What are people building with OpenClaw?\n\nThe two biggest Hacker News threads about OpenClaw ([Ask HN: Any real OpenClaw users?](https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=46838946) and [OpenClaw is changing my life](https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=46931805)) are full of real use cases:\n\n- One user runs six agents as a personal staff: a PA that supervises the others, one handling Twitter growth, another scouting job listings, and a fourth paper-trading crypto with a claimed 77% win rate\n- A designer built mold-making software (plastic to silicone to plaster to clay) in about 12 hours\n- Someone migrated a Python 2.x home automation setup to Python 3 in 20 minutes\n- Others are managing 15,000+ email inboxes, building location-based games you play by walking through parks, and generating Lilypond music arrangements\n\nThe consensus among experienced engineers in the threads is that OpenClaw works best for greenfield projects and boilerplate. It struggles with large existing codebases and complex refactoring. Several commenters warned about token costs ($200-400/month for heavy use) and flagged suspicious testimonial patterns in the threads themselves.\n\nThe skills ecosystem sits at 5,705+ community-built skills on ClawHub, though a curated list ([awesome-openclaw-skills](https://github.com/VoltAgent/awesome-openclaw-skills)) filters that down to 3,002 after removing 1,180 spam accounts, 672 crypto schemes, 492 duplicates, and 396 flagged as malicious. The 27 remaining categories range from DevOps and smart home control to an AI dating assistant called flirtingbots.\n\n## How I've been using ClawdBot\n\nI connected OpenClaw to Telegram and immediately noticed something I didn't expect. Telegram bots support slash commands natively, so OpenClaw surfaces more than 20 of them by default: `/new`, `/reset`, `/status`, `/context`, `/think`, and more. It feels closer to using the terminal interface (the most powerful way to run OpenClaw) than a typical chat.\n\nOnce I got comfortable with the basics, I started turning it into a personal assistant. I gave it access to Gmail, Google Calendar, and Drive through the built-in skills, so I can manage client work and communication from my phone.\n\nThe Telegram setup has a side benefit that it's always there. If I'm texting friends and someone mentions a birthday or a dinner, I switch to my OpenClaw chat, tell it to create a reminder, and go back to the conversation. No opening a separate app.\n\nI also set up heartbeats, which are cron jobs that run on whatever schedule you define. Mine fires every morning with a summary of who's waiting for an email response and what's on my calendar for the day.\n\nBut the real effect OpenClaw has had on my life is as an accountability partner. I have ADHD and struggle with executive dysfunction, which means severe time blindness and a hard time meeting deadlines. So I built a system for it.\n\nUsing Claude Code (not OpenClaw; my $200 Max account covers coding), I built a local Mac agent called Narc. It captures periodic screenshots of all my displays along with window titles and browser history, then pushes everything to a shared S3 bucket.\n\nOpenClaw polls that bucket on a heartbeat schedule. When it finds me doing something I shouldn't be during an enforced work session (which it can see on my calendar), it confronts me on Telegram. I'm currently working on hooking it up to my actual Telegram account so it can message friends and family when I'm caught slacking. Consequences with witnesses.\n\n![Narc accountability system data flow showing MacBook capturing screenshots to S3, OpenClaw on EC2 polling the bucket, checking Google Calendar for work sessions, and sending confrontation messages through Telegram](/images/blog/openclaw-firecrawl-guide/narc-accountability-system-flow.webp)\n\nThere's also a library of community-built skills you can install. Here are a few from the curated list:\n\n- [pndr](https://github.com/openclaw/skills/tree/main/skills/dgershman/pndr) - all-in-one productivity: idea capture, task management, journaling, habit tracking\n- [cognitive-memory](https://github.com/openclaw/skills/tree/main/skills/icemilo414/cognitive-memory) - multi-store memory that mimics human short-term and long-term patterns\n- [moltbot-ha](https://github.com/openclaw/skills/tree/main/skills/iamvaleriofantozzi/moltbot-ha) - controls Home Assistant devices through natural language\n- [voice-reply](https://github.com/openclaw/skills/tree/main/skills/stolot0mt0m/voice-reply) - local text-to-speech using Piper voices, no cloud API needed\n- [quests](https://github.com/openclaw/skills/tree/main/skills/poloio/quests) - guides you through complex multi-step processes like buying a house or filing taxes\n\nBut one thing that needs more than a skill install is web search. OpenClaw's `web_search` doesn't work at all without a provider. The options are a [Brave Search API](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/brave-search-api-alternatives) key or Firecrawl. I went with Firecrawl because Brave gives you links, and when the agent tries to actually read those pages, it runs into 403 errors and bot protection on most modern sites.
