Firecrawl returns the actual page content with [search results](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/introducing-search-endpoint), so the agent skips that problem entirely.\n\n## Giving your agent live web data with Firecrawl\n\n![Comparison of two Firecrawl integration methods for OpenClaw: API key config as an HTTP fallback for blocked pages versus the full Firecrawl skill with search, scrape, crawl, and map capabilities](/images/blog/openclaw-firecrawl-guide/firecrawl-api-key-vs-skill-integration.webp)\n\nThe simplest way to add Firecrawl is dropping your API key into the config file. This gives your agent a fallback for JS-heavy and bot-protected pages. For web search, crawling, and structured extraction, you install the full Firecrawl skill through Telegram in about 30 seconds.\n\n### Adding the API key\n\nOpen `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` and add your Firecrawl API key:\n\n```json\n{\n  \"tools\": {\n    \"web\": {\n      \"fetch\": {\n        \"firecrawl\": {\n          \"apiKey\": \"fc-YOUR-API-KEY\"\n        }\n      }\n    }\n  }\n}\n```\n\nWith this config, your agent falls back to Firecrawl whenever a page can't be read with a standard HTTP request. JavaScript-rendered sites, anti-bot protection, paywalls: they all go through Firecrawl instead of returning garbage or a 403. You can tune `maxAgeMs` to control how long cached results stay fresh (default is 2 days).\n\nThis covers individual page reads. For web search, [site crawling](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/mastering-the-crawl-endpoint-in-firecrawl), and [structured extraction](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/introducing-extract-open-beta), you want the full skill.\n\nFor a complete breakdown of how OpenClaw's web_search and web_fetch tools work together, how to configure each search provider, and when to use Firecrawl's search instead, see our [OpenClaw web search guide](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/openclaw-web-search).\n\n### Installing the Firecrawl skill\n\nYou don't need to touch the terminal. Just text your agent:\n\n![Telegram chat showing OpenClaw agent installing the Firecrawl skill from ClawHub with a single message command](/images/blog/openclaw-firecrawl-guide/firecrawl-skill-install.webp)\n\nClaw finds the available Firecrawl skills on [ClawHub](https://clawhub.com), asks which one you want, and installs it. The `firecrawl-skills` option gives your agent scrape, search, crawl, and map under the hood. It authenticates automatically if you have `FIRECRAWL_API_KEY` set in your environment.\n\nFrom here, you just talk to your agent like normal. When it needs to read a webpage, it [scrapes through a real browser](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/mastering-firecrawl-scrape-endpoint) instead of failing on JavaScript. When it needs to research something, it searches the web and pulls back full page content instead of just links. When you point it at a documentation site, it can crawl every page and map the structure.\n\nHere's what that looks like in practice. I asked my agent to compare pricing across three SaaS products:\n\n![OpenClaw agent comparing SaaS pricing by scraping multiple websites through Firecrawl and returning results in a single Telegram message](/images/blog/openclaw-firecrawl-guide/firecrawl-pricing-query.webp)\n\nIt searched the web, scraped each pricing page, and put the results together in one message. No URLs needed, no 403 errors, no browser tabs.\n\nYou can learn more at our [Guide to the Firecrawl Skill and CLI](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/introducing-firecrawl-skill-and-cli).\n\n### Browser Sandbox: moving browser automation off your machine\n\nThere's another bottleneck that shows up once you start running more ambitious workflows: the browser itself.\n\nOpenClaw's default setup drives your local browser. That works for simple tasks, but two problems surface quickly. First, your agent is sharing an environment with your real browsing state — a real security concern. Second, the moment you try to run a few sessions in parallel, your machine becomes the bottleneck. RAM spikes, the agent slows down, and runs get flaky.\n\nFirecrawl's [Browser Sandbox](https://docs.firecrawl.dev/features/browser) solves this by moving browser work into a secure, remote, disposable environment. No local Chromium installs. No driver setup. Your agent can spin up a browser on demand — one session or dozens — without tying the workload to the machine it's running on. Your OpenClaw agent can run on a free-tier EC2 instance or a Raspberry Pi while the browsing happens elsewhere.\n\n[Setting it up is a single command](https://x.com/nickscamara_/status/2024226351369376211):\n\n```bash\nnpx -y firecrawl-cli init --browser\n```\n\nThis installs the Firecrawl CLI, opens a browser to authenticate, and installs the skill. Once that's done, you can tell your agent:\n\n\u003e Use Firecrawl Browser Sandbox to open Hacker News and get me the top 5 news of the day and the first 10 comments on each\n\nUnder the hood, the `firecrawl browser` commands use an agent-browser interface to execute intent-level commands (`open`, `click`, `fill`, `snapshot`, `scrape`) inside the sandbox instead of generating and debugging Playwright code. What your agent actually runs looks like this:\n\n```bash\nfirecrawl browser \"open https://news.ycombinator.com\"\nfirecrawl browser \"snapshot\"\nfirecrawl browser \"scrape\"\nfirecrawl browser close\n```\n\nA few things worth knowing about how this works:\n\n- **Auto-session**: the shorthand form (`firecrawl browser \"...\"`) will auto-launch a sandbox session if one isn't active, so the agent doesn't need to manage session lifecycle\n- **Token efficiency**: the agent gets back artifacts (snapshot/extracted content) instead of raw DOM logs, and uses the filesystem to save fetched pages rather than loading them all into context at once\n- **Full toolkit**: scrape, search, and browser automation all through a single CLI\n\nThis is especially useful for the EC2 setup described above. Your EC2 instance handles the agent logic while Browser Sandbox handles the actual browsing — each doing what it's optimized for.\n\n*Watch this video where we evaluate how to use Firecrawl inside of Openclaw to easily extract data from complex, dynamically rendered websites. We walk through a quick, secure Docker setup for Openclaw and connect OpenRouter to access different LLMs.*\n\n*After demonstrating how the default web fetch struggles with JavaScript elements like pagination and \"see more\" buttons, we evaluate the Firecrawl browser feature as the solution. By installing the Firecrawl CLI, we show exactly how your AI agents can navigate pages, click buttons, and scrape the full data you need without hitting rendering roadblocks.*\n\n\u003ciframe width=\"560\" height=\"315\" src=\"https://www.youtube.com/embed/KkvcLUXWXe8?si=0ezZnxeNB33mXd6Y\" title=\"YouTube video player\" frameborder=\"0\" allow=\"accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share\" referrerpolicy=\"strict-origin-when-cross-origin\" allowfullscreen\u003e\u003c/iframe\u003e\n\n## What I learned about OpenClaw the hard way\n\nI started running OpenClaw on my MacBook because I don't have a desktop. That lasted about a week. Reports about OpenClaw's security vulnerabilities started piling up (exposed control panels, one-click RCE, credentials stored in plaintext), and running an agent with full system access on my primary machine didn't sit right.\n\nOn top of that, keeping a laptop open 24/7 isn't practical. Battery health, sleep settings, lid behavior. I uninstalled completely.\n\nThe fix was deploying to AWS. I spun up an [EC2 instance](https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/pricing/on-demand/) (t4g.small, about $15/month) and moved everything there. The agent was now up around the clock, and my accountability system with Narc could poll S3 without me worrying about my MacBook sleeping mid-session.\n\nIf you're considering a Mac Mini for this (the common recommendation), I'd argue EC2 is a better deal unless you need the macOS-specific features. A t4g.small with an existing Anthropic subscription runs under $30/month total.\n\nSetting up the EC2 was the hardest part because you install everything from scratch: Node.js, OpenClaw, systemd services, security groups. Here's a single script that handles the full deployment. It assumes your AWS credentials are configured locally with the right permissions:\n\n```bash\n#!/bin/bash\n# deploy-openclaw.sh\nset -euo pipefail\n\nREGION=\"us-east-1\"\nINSTANCE_TYPE=\"t4g.small\"  # 2 vCPU, 2GB RAM, ~$15/month\nKEY_NAME=\"openclaw-key\"\n\n# SSH key pair\naws ec2 create-key-pair \\\n  --key-name \"$KEY_NAME\" \\\n  --key-type ed25519 \\\n  --region \"$REGION\" \\\n  --query 'KeyMaterial' --output text \u003e \"${KEY_NAME}.pem\"\nchmod 400 \"${KEY_NAME}.pem\"\n\n# Security group: SSH only, gateway port stays closed\nSG_ID=$(aws ec2 create-security-group \\\n  --group-name openclaw-sg \\\n  --description \"OpenClaw server\" \\\n  --region \"$REGION\" \\\n  --query 'GroupId' --output text)\n\nMY_IP=$(curl -s https://checkip.amazonaws.com)\naws ec2 authorize-security-group-ingress \\\n  --group-id \"$SG_ID\" \\\n  --protocol tcp --port 22 \\\n  --cidr \"${MY_IP}/32\" \\\n  --region \"$REGION\"\n\n# Ubuntu 24.04 ARM64\nAMI_ID=$(aws ssm get-parameter \\\n  --name /aws/service/canonical/ubuntu/server/noble/stable/current/arm64/hvm/ebs-gp3/ami-id \\\n  --query 'Parameter.Value' --output text \\\n  --region \"$REGION\")\n\n# Launch\nINSTANCE_ID=$(aws ec2 run-instances \\\n  --image-id \"$AMI_ID\" \\\n  --instance-type \"$INSTANCE_TYPE\" \\\n  --key-name \"$KEY_NAME\" \\\n  --security-group-ids \"$SG_ID\" \\\n  --block-device-mappings 'DeviceName=/dev/sda1,Ebs={VolumeSize=30,VolumeType=gp3}' \\\n  --tag-specifications 'ResourceType=instance,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=openclaw-server}]' \\\n  --region \"$REGION\" \\\n  --query 'Instances[0].InstanceId' --output text)\n\naws ec2 wait instance-running --instance-ids \"$INSTANCE_ID\" --region \"$REGION\"\n\nEC2_IP=$(aws ec2 describe-instances \\\n  --instance-ids \"$INSTANCE_ID\" \\\n  --region \"$REGION\" \\\n  --query 'Reservations[0].Instances[0].PublicIpAddress' --output text)\n\necho \"Instance up at ${EC2_IP}. Waiting for SSH...\"\nsleep 30\n\n# Install Node.js, OpenClaw, and start the gateway\nssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no -i \"${KEY_NAME}.pem\" ubuntu@\"${EC2_IP}\" \u003c\u003c 'REMOTE'\nset -euo pipefail\n\nsudo apt-get update \u0026\u0026 sudo apt-get upgrade -y\n\n# Node.js 22 via NVM\ncurl -o- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nvm-sh/nvm/v0.40.1/install.sh | bash\nexport NVM_DIR=\"$HOME/.nvm\"\n[ -s \"$NVM_DIR/nvm.sh\" ] \u0026\u0026 . \"$NVM_DIR/nvm.sh\"\nnvm install 22\n\nnpm install -g openclaw@latest\n\n# Headless systemd support (required on EC2)\nsudo loginctl enable-linger ubuntu\nexport XDG_RUNTIME_DIR=/run/user/$(id -u)\necho 'export XDG_RUNTIME_DIR=/run/user/$(id -u)' \u003e\u003e ~/.bashrc\n\n# Onboard and start\nopenclaw onboard --non-interactive --gateway-bind loopback --skip-daemon --skip-skills\nopenclaw gateway install --force\nopenclaw gateway start\n\necho \"OpenClaw running. Set your API keys and connect Telegram.\"\nREMOTE\n\necho \"SSH: ssh -i ${KEY_NAME}.pem ubuntu@${EC2_IP}\"\necho \"Tunnel: ssh -L 18789:127.0.0.1:18789 -i ${KEY_NAME}.pem ubuntu@${EC2_IP}\"\n```\n\nOnce the instance is up, SSH in, set your `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` (or integration token) and `FIRECRAWL_API_KEY` in `~/.bashrc`, then connect Telegram with `openclaw channels login --channel telegram`. The gateway port (18789) stays closed to the public. You access it through an SSH tunnel if you ever need the web interface.\n\nAfter a few weeks of running, I hit a problem. Claw's responses started getting slower and slower. The typing indicator would hang for minutes, even on short messages. Starting a fresh conversation with `/new` didn't help. I ran `openclaw doctor --deep` and did a full audit of the system.\n\nThe cause: without regular cleanup, heartbeat cron jobs and conversation history accumulate stale data. OpenClaw was injecting all of it into every new thread, burning through my Sonnet 4.5 account limits on context that didn't matter anymore.\n\nI was very glad to have used a Claude integration token instead of a raw API key. With per-token billing, that kind of silent context bloat would have been an expensive surprise.\n\n## Conclusion\n\nOpenClaw went from a one-hour WhatsApp relay to 175,000+ GitHub stars in under three months. Peter Steinberger joined OpenAI on February 15, 2026, and the project is moving to an open-source foundation with OpenAI's continued support.\n\nFor me, it started as a curiosity and turned into something I use every day. The accountability system, morning email digests, calendar reminders mid-conversation, Firecrawl-powered web research... all running on a $15/month EC2 instance that I set up in an afternoon. The rough edges are real (stale data buildup, security concerns, the triple rebrand), but nothing that made me want to stop using it.\n\n"])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"a0:T7281,"])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"\nWeb scraping code tends to rot. You spend hours finding right CSS selectors, then the site redesigns and everything breaks. The fix is usually more brittle code on top of brittle code.\n\nAgents offer a different model. Give an LLM the right tools and it can figure out how to extract what you need from a page, even when the structure changes. The reasoning happens at runtime instead of being baked into your selectors.\n\nWe're going to build one of these agents using [LangGraph](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/langgraph-startup-validator-tutorial) for the agent loop and Firecrawl for the actual scraping. Firecrawl handles the annoying parts like [JavaScript rendering](https://www.firecrawl.dev/glossary/web-scraping-apis/what-is-javascript-rendering-web-scraping) and bot detection, returning clean markdown. LangGraph gives us a simple way to wire up tools and let the model decide when to call them. The finished agent runs in the terminal and responds to plain English commands for scraping, screenshots, [structured extraction](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/data-extraction-using-llms), [web search](https://www.firecrawl.dev/glossary/web-search-apis/what-is-web-search-api), and [documentation crawling](https://www.firecrawl.dev/glossary/web-crawling-apis/what-is-web-crawling-api).\n\n## TL;DR\n\n- **Build a ReAct-style agent in ~300 lines** using LangGraph tasks and a simple loop\n- **Use Firecrawl for the heavy lifting**: scrape, screenshot, extract, search, and crawl without managing browsers\n- **Expose six tools to the LLM** so it can decide what to call based on plain English requests\n- **Add an iteration limit** to avoid infinite tool-call loops in production\n- **Use Firecrawl `/agent` as the fast alternative** when you want structured data without custom tool wiring\n\n## What is a web scraping agent and why build one?\n\nA traditional scraping script does exactly what you tell it. When you need data from a new page, you write new code. When you want to combine scraping with a web search, you add more code. The script grows with every requirement, and so does the maintenance burden.\n\nAn agent works differently. You define a set of tools once, then the LLM figures out how to combine them based on what you ask. Searching for competitor pricing pages, scraping each result, and pulling prices into JSON becomes a single request instead of a custom pipeline you have to build and debug.\n\nThe stack for the tool we'll build has three parts:\n\n- LangGraph's functional API provides the agent loop through `@task` and `@entrypoint` decorators\n- The ReAct pattern (reason, act, observe, repeat) fits in about 20 lines without complex state graph definitions\n- Firecrawl's Python SDK wraps scraping, screenshots, structured extraction, search, and crawling into single function calls with no browser drivers to manage.\n\nRich gives us formatted terminal output, markdown rendering, and status spinners so the agent feels polished without much effort. The entire agent comes out to under 300 lines of Python.\n\n![Diagram comparing traditional web scraping workflow with repetitive code maintenance cycle versus agent-based approach with flexible tool composition around a central LLM](/images/blog/web-scraping-agent-langgraph-firecrawl/traditional_vs_agent.webp)\n\n## Prerequisites and setup\n\nBefore writing any code, you need a few things in place: Python 3.10+, API keys for Firecrawl and OpenAI, and the right packages installed. We'll use `uv` for dependency management since it's faster than pip and handles virtual environments automatically.\n\n### Installing dependencies\n\nCreate a new project directory and initialize it with uv:\n\n```bash\nmkdir scraping-agent \u0026\u0026 cd scraping-agent\nuv init\n```\n\nInstall the required packages:\n\n```bash\nuv add firecrawl langgraph langchain-openai python-dotenv rich\n```\n\nThis gives you:\n\n- `firecrawl`: The Python SDK for Firecrawl's scraping, crawling, and extraction APIs\n- `langgraph`: LangChain's framework for building stateful agents\n- `langchain-openai`: OpenAI integration for LangChain\n- `python-dotenv`: Load environment variables from a `.env` file\n- `rich`: Terminal formatting for a nicer chat interface\n\n### Getting your API keys\n\nYou need two API keys:\n\n1. **Firecrawl API key**: Sign up at [firecrawl.dev](https://firecrawl.dev) and grab your key from the dashboard. The free tier includes 500 credits, more than enough for this tutorial.\n2. **OpenAI API key**: Get one from [platform.openai.com](https://platform.openai.com) and add some balance to your account. We'll use GPT-5 as the reasoning model, but expect costs under $1 for following along with this tutorial.\n\nCreate a `.env` file in your project root:\n\n```bash\nFIRECRAWL_API_KEY=fc-your-key-here\nOPENAI_API_KEY=sk-your-key-here\n```\n\n### Project structure\n\nYour directory should look like this:\n\n```plaintext\nscraping-agent/\n├── .env\n├── .python-version\n├── pyproject.toml\n└── main.py          # We'll build this next\n```\n\nYou may also have a README.md and lockfile depending on your version of uv. With the environment ready, we can start defining the tools our agent will use.\n\n## Defining the Firecrawl tools\n\nLangChain's `@tool` decorator turns regular Python functions into tools that an LLM can call. The decorator reads the function's docstring to understand what the tool does and when to use it. Our agent needs six tools: five that wrap Firecrawl endpoints and one for Python code execution.\n\n![Architecture diagram showing three layers of the web scraping agent: LLM agent layer for reasoning, LangChain tools layer with six tools, and Firecrawl API endpoints for execution](/images/blog/web-scraping-agent-langgraph-firecrawl/tool_architecture.webp)\n\nAdd the following code to the main.py file:\n\n```python\n# Add these imports with the other imports at the top of the file\nimport os\nimport urllib.request\n\nfrom dotenv import load_dotenv\nfrom firecrawl import Firecrawl\nfrom langchain_core.tools import tool\nfrom langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI\n```\n\n```python\nload_dotenv()\n\nfc = Firecrawl(api_key=os.environ.get(\"FIRECRAWL_API_KEY\"))\nmodel = ChatOpenAI(\n    model=\"gpt-5\",\n    api_key=os.environ.get(\"OPENAI_API_KEY\"),\n    temperature=0,\n)\n```\n\n### scrape_markdown\n\n```python\n@tool\ndef scrape_markdown(url: str, main_only: bool = False) -\u003e str:\n    \"\"\"Scrape a URL and return clean markdown content.\n\n    Args:\n        url: The URL to scrape\n        main_only: If True, exclude navigation, footers, and sidebars\n    \"\"\"\n    result = fc.scrape(\n        url, formats=[\"markdown\"], only_main_content=main_only if main_only else None\n    )\n    return result.markdown\n```\n\nFirecrawl's [scrape endpoint](https://docs.firecrawl.dev/features/scrape) handles JavaScript rendering and bot detection, returning clean markdown. The `only_main_content` flag strips nav bars, footers, and sidebars when you just want the article body.\n\nFor a complete guide to the scrape endpoint, see [Mastering Firecrawl's Scrape Endpoint](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/mastering-firecrawl-scrape-endpoint).\n\n### take_screenshot\n\n```python\n@tool\ndef take_screenshot(url: str, output_path: str) -\u003e str:\n    \"\"\"Capture a full-page screenshot of a URL and save it locally.\n\n    Args:\n        url: The URL to screenshot\n        output_path: Local file path to save the screenshot (PNG)\n    \"\"\"\n    result = fc.scrape(url, formats=[\"screenshot\"])\n    urllib.request.urlretrieve(result.screenshot, output_path)\n    return f\"Screenshot saved to {output_path}\"\n```\n\nSame scrape endpoint, different format. Firecrawl returns a cloud URL for the image, and `urllib` downloads it to the path the agent specifies.\n\n### extract_data\n\n```python\n@tool\ndef extract_data(url: str, json_schema: dict, prompt: str = None) -\u003e dict:\n    \"\"\"Extract structured data from a URL\n\n    Args:\n        url: The URL to extract data from\n        json_schema: JSON schema defining the structure of data to extract\n        prompt: Prompt to guide the extraction\n    \"\"\"\n    format_spec = {\"type\": \"json\", \"schema\": json_schema}\n    if prompt:\n        format_spec[\"prompt\"] = prompt\n\n    result = fc.scrape(url, formats=[format_spec])\n    return result.json\n```\n\nThe [agent endpoint](https://docs.firecrawl.dev/features/agent) is LLM-powered. You describe your requirement in a simple prompt and it gathers the structured data for you. URLs are optional. You can also pass a JSON schema like `{\"type\": \"object\", \"properties\": {\"product_name\": {\"type\": \"string\"}, \"price\": {\"type\": \"number\"}}}` and Firecrawl pulls matching data from the page.\n\nFor more details on capabilities and parameters, see the [agent documentation](https://docs.firecrawl.dev/features/agent).\n\n### search_web\n\n```python\n@tool\ndef search_web(query: str, limit: int = 5) -\u003e str:\n    \"\"\"Search the web and return results with content.\n\n    Args:\n        query: The search query\n        limit: Maximum number of results (default: 5)\n    \"\"\"\n    results = fc.search(query, limit=limit)\n    web_results = getattr(results, \"web\", []) or []\n    output = [\n        f\"## {getattr(r, 'title', 'Untitled')}\\nURL: {getattr(r, 'url', '')}\\n{getattr(r, 'description', '')}\"\n        for r in web_results\n    ]\n    return \"\\n---\\n\".join(output) if output else \"No results found.\"\n```\n\nThe [search endpoint](https://docs.firecrawl.dev/features/search) returns titles, URLs, and descriptions. The agent uses this to find pages before deciding which ones to scrape. For a deeper walkthrough, see [Mastering Firecrawl Search Endpoint](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/mastering-firecrawl-search-endpoint).\n\n### crawl_docs\n\n```python\n@tool\ndef crawl_docs(url: str, limit: int = 30) -\u003e str:\n    \"\"\"Crawl a documentation site and return page summaries.\n\n    Args:\n        url: The starting URL to crawl\n        limit: Maximum number of pages to crawl (default: 30)\n    \"\"\"\n    result = fc.crawl(\n        url,\n        limit=limit,\n        scrape_options={\"formats\": [\"markdown\"], \"onlyMainContent\": True},\n    )\n    pages = result.data\n    summaries = [\n        f\"- {page.metadata.title if hasattr(page, 'metadata') and page.metadata else 'Untitled'}: {getattr(page, 'url', '')}\"\n        for page in pages[:10]\n    ]\n    return f\"Crawled {len(pages)} pages:\\n\" + \"\\n\".join(summaries)\n```\n\nThe [crawl endpoint](https://docs.firecrawl.dev/features/crawl) follows links from a starting URL and scrapes multiple pages. Built for documentation sites, and you can take this further by [turning crawled docs into a RAG-powered agent](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/build-documentation-agent-langgraph-firecrawl). This version returns summaries, but the agent could use `run_python` to save full page contents to disk.\n\nFor more advanced crawling techniques, see [Mastering the Crawl Endpoint](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/mastering-the-crawl-endpoint-in-firecrawl).\n\n### run_python\n\n```python\nimport io\nfrom contextlib import redirect_stdout, redirect_stderr\n\n@tool\ndef run_python(code: str) -\u003e str:\n    \"\"\"Execute Python code for file operations, data processing, and analysis.\n\n    Use this tool to:\n    - Save scraped data to files (JSON, CSV, text)\n    - Process and transform data\n    - Read files from disk\n    - Perform calculations or data analysis\n\n    Args:\n        code: Python code to execute. Use print() to output results.\n    \"\"\"\n    stdout_capture = io.StringIO()\n    stderr_capture = io.StringIO()\n\n    try:\n        with redirect_stdout(stdout_capture), redirect_stderr(stderr_capture):\n            exec(code, {\"__builtins__\": __builtins__})\n\n        stdout_output = stdout_capture.getvalue()\n        stderr_output = stderr_capture.getvalue()\n\n        result = \"\"\n        if stdout_output:\n            result += stdout_output\n        if stderr_output:\n            result += f\"\\nStderr:\\n{stderr_output}\"\n\n        return (\n            result.strip()\n            if result.strip()\n            else \"Code executed successfully (no output).\"\n        )\n\n    except Exception as e:\n        return f\"Error: {type(e).__name__}: {str(e)}\"\n```\n\nNot a Firecrawl tool, but it rounds out the agent. Executes arbitrary Python and returns whatever gets printed. Uses `exec()` with full builtins access, which is fine running locally but not something to expose over a network.\n\nSix tools total: scrape, screenshot, agent, search, crawl, and code execution. Next we wire them into the agent loop.\n\n## Building the LangGraph agent\n\nWith the tools defined, we need to wire them into an agent loop. [LangGraph's functional API](https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/concepts/functional_api/) makes this straightforward with two decorators: `@task` for individual operations and `@entrypoint` for the main agent function.\n\n```python\nfrom langchain_core.messages import ToolMessage\nfrom langgraph.func import entrypoint, task\nfrom langgraph.graph.message import add_messages\n```\n\nFirst, collect the tools into a list and bind them to the model. Binding tells the LLM what tools exist and how to call them.\n\n```python\ntools = [\n    scrape_markdown,\n    take_screenshot,\n    extract_data,\n    search_web,\n    crawl_docs,\n    run_python,\n]\ntools_by_name = {t.name: t for t in tools}\n\nmodel_with_tools = model.bind_tools(tools)\n```\n\n### The call_model task\n\nThe `@task` decorator marks a function as a unit of work that LangGraph can track and execute. Our first task calls the LLM with a system prompt and the conversation history.\n\n```python\n@task\ndef call_model(messages: list):\n    \"\"\"Call the LLM with the current message history.\"\"\"\n    system_message = {\n        \"role\": \"system\",\n        \"content\": \"\"\"You are a web scraping assistant with access to Firecrawl and Python tools.\n\nAvailable tools:\n- scrape_markdown: Get clean markdown content from any URL\n- take_screenshot: Capture screenshots of web pages\n- extract_data: Extract structured data using JSON schemas\n- search_web: Search the web for information\n- crawl_docs: Crawl documentation sites\n- run_python: Execute Python code for file operations, data processing, saving results\n\nWhen users ask to scrape, search, or extract data from the web, use the appropriate tool.\nUse run_python to save data to files, process results, or perform calculations.\nBe concise in your responses. When showing scraped content, summarize the main points.\"\"\",\n    }\n    return model_with_tools.invoke([system_message] + messages)\n```\n\nThe system prompt tells the model what it can do and how to behave. Listing the tools here reinforces what the model learned from the tool bindings.\n\n### The call_tool task\n\nWhen the model decides to use a tool, we need to execute it and return the result. The second task handles this.\n\n```python\n@task\ndef call_tool(tool_call: dict):\n    \"\"\"Execute a single tool call.\"\"\"\n    tool = tools_by_name[tool_call[\"name\"]]\n    try:\n        result = tool.invoke(tool_call[\"args\"])\n        if isinstance(result, str) and len(result) \u003e 5000:\n            result = result[:5000] + \"\\n\\n[... truncated ...]\"\n        return ToolMessage(content=str(result), tool_call_id=tool_call[\"id\"])\n    except Exception as e:\n        return ToolMessage(content=f\"Error: {str(e)}\", tool_call_id=tool_call[\"id\"])\n```\n\nThe function looks up the tool by name, invokes it with the arguments the model provided, and wraps the result in a `ToolMessage`. We truncate long outputs to keep context windows manageable. Errors get caught and returned as messages so the model can try a different approach instead of crashing.\n\n### The agent loop\n\nThe `@entrypoint` decorator marks the main function that orchestrates everything. This is where the ReAct pattern lives: the model reasons about what to do, acts by calling tools, observes the results, and repeats until it has an answer.\n\n```python\n@entrypoint()\ndef agent(messages: list):\n    \"\"\"ReAct agent loop: model -\u003e tools -\u003e model -\u003e ... -\u003e response\"\"\"\n    llm_response = call_model(messages).result()\n\n    while True:\n        if not llm_response.tool_calls:\n            break\n\n        # Show which tools are being called\n        for tc in llm_response.tool_calls:\n            console.print(f\"  [dim]Calling {tc['name']}...[/dim]\")\n\n        # Execute tools in parallel\n        tool_futures = [call_tool(tc) for tc in llm_response.tool_calls]\n        tool_results = [f.result() for f in tool_futures]\n\n        # Update message history\n        messages = add_messages(messages, [llm_response, *tool_results])\n\n        # Call model again with tool results\n        llm_response = call_model(messages).result()\n\n    return llm_response\n```\n\nThe loop starts by calling the model. If the response includes tool calls, we execute them all (in parallel via task futures), add the results to the message history, and call the model again. When the model responds without requesting any tools, the loop breaks and we return the final response.\n\n![Circular flow diagram showing the ReAct agent loop with four stages: Reason, Act, Observe, and Decide, cycling until the agent has enough information to return a response](/images/blog/web-scraping-agent-langgraph-firecrawl/react_loop.webp)\n\nThe `add_messages` helper from LangGraph handles message deduplication and proper ordering. The `.result()` calls block until each task completes.\n\nYou'll notice a `console.print` call that we haven't defined yet. That's the Rich console object, which we'll set up in the terminal interface along with the rest of the chat loop.\n\n## Creating the terminal interface\n\nThe agent logic is done, but we need a way to interact with it. [Rich](https://rich.readthedocs.io/) gives us a polished terminal experience with minimal code: formatted prompts, status spinners, and markdown rendering.\n\n```python\n# Add these to the top of the file\nfrom rich.console import Console\nfrom rich.markdown import Markdown\nfrom rich.panel import Panel\nfrom rich.prompt import Prompt\n\nconsole = Console()\n```\n\nThe `Console` object is Rich's main interface. All output goes through it, and it handles things like color support detection and terminal width automatically.\n\n### The main function\n\nThe terminal interface lives in a `main()` function that displays a welcome message and runs the chat loop.\n\n```python\ndef main():\n    \"\"\"Run the interactive terminal interface.\"\"\"\n    console.print(\n        Panel(\n            \"[bold]Web Scraping Agent[/bold]\\n\\n\"\n            \"I can scrape websites, take screenshots, extract structured data,\\n\"\n            \"search the web, crawl documentation sites, and save results to files.\\n\\n\"\n            \"[dim]Type /quit to exit[/dim]\",\n            border_style=\"blue\",\n        )\n    )\n\n    messages = []\n```\n\nRich uses a markup syntax similar to BBCode. Tags like `[bold]` and `[dim]` wrap text to apply styles, and you close them with `[/bold]` or `[/dim]`. The `Panel` class draws a box around content, and `border_style` sets its color.\n\nThe `messages` list holds the conversation history. It starts empty and grows as the user and agent exchange messages.\n\n### The chat loop\n\nThe rest of `main()` is a standard input loop with some Rich polish.\n\n```python\n    # Continue in main function\n    while True:\n        try:\n            user_input = Prompt.ask(\"\\n[bold green]You[/bold green]\")\n        except (KeyboardInterrupt, EOFError):\n            console.print(\"\\n[dim]Goodbye![/dim]\")\n            break\n\n        if user_input.lower() in [\"/quit\", \"/exit\", \"/q\"]:\n            console.print(\"[dim]Goodbye![/dim]\")\n            break\n\n        if not user_input.strip():\n            continue\n\n        messages.append({\"role\": \"user\", \"content\": user_input})\n\n        with console.status(\"[bold blue]Thinking...[/bold blue]\"):\n            response = agent.invoke(messages)\n\n        messages.append({\"role\": \"assistant\", \"content\": response.content})\n\n        console.print(\"\\n[bold blue]Agent[/bold blue]\")\n        if response.content:\n            try:\n                console.print(Markdown(response.content))\n            except Exception:\n                console.print(response.content)\n\n\nif __name__ == \"__main__\":\n    main()\n```\n\n`Prompt.ask()` displays a styled prompt and waits for input. We catch `KeyboardInterrupt` and `EOFError` so Ctrl+C and Ctrl+D exit gracefully instead of dumping a traceback.\n\nThe `console.status()` context manager shows an animated spinner with the message \"Thinking...\" while the agent runs. It disappears automatically when the block exits.\n\nFor the agent's response, we try to render it as `Markdown` for nicer formatting of headers, lists, and code blocks. If that fails for any reason, we fall back to plain text.\n\nRun the agent with:\n\n```bash\nuv run python main.py\n```\n\nYou'll see the welcome panel, and then you can start asking it to scrape pages, take screenshots, or search the web.\n\n![Terminal screenshot showing the web scraping agent welcome panel with blue border listing available capabilities like scraping, screenshots, and data extraction](/images/blog/web-scraping-agent-langgraph-firecrawl/agent-terminal-welcome.webp)\n\n## Testing the agent\n\nWith the code complete, I ran a few tasks to see how the agent handles real-world requests.\n\n### Research task: comparing web scraping APIs\n\nMy first prompt asked the agent to do competitive research:\n\n```plaintext\nI'm evaluating web scraping APIs. Search for comparisons between Firecrawl,\nScrapingBee, and Browserless, then summarize the pros and cons of each\n```\n\n![Terminal output showing the agent executing search_web and scrape_markdown tools to compare web scraping APIs and return structured results](/images/blog/web-scraping-agent-langgraph-firecrawl/test-api-comparison.webp)\n\nThe agent orchestrated multiple tool calls on its own. It started with `search_web` to find comparison articles and documentation pages, then used `scrape_markdown` to pull content from the most relevant results. The final output was a structured comparison with pros, cons, and source links for each service.\n\nAgents shine over traditional scripts here. I didn't specify which sites to check or how to structure the output. The model figured out it needed current information from multiple sources, gathered it, and synthesized a useful summary.\n\n### Documentation lookup: LangGraph quick reference\n\nNext, I wanted to test the crawl functionality:\n\n```plaintext\nCrawl the LangGraph documentation and create a quick reference of the main\ndecorators and when to use them\n```\n\n![Terminal output showing the agent using crawl_docs to index LangGraph documentation and generate a quick reference guide](/images/blog/web-scraping-agent-langgraph-firecrawl/test-langgraph-docs.webp)\n\nThe agent used `crawl_docs` once, then supplemented with `search_web` and `scrape_markdown` to fill in details. The result was a detailed reference covering `@entrypoint` and `@task` decorators with usage examples, parameter lists, and links to the official docs.\n\nI then asked it to save this as a reference file:\n\n```plaintext\nSave that information as reference.md\n```\n\n![Terminal output showing the agent calling run_python to save the LangGraph reference as a markdown file](/images/blog/web-scraping-agent-langgraph-firecrawl/test-save-reference.webp)\n\nThe agent called `run_python` to write the file, confirming that message history was working correctly since it remembered the previous response.\n\n### A lesson in iteration limits\n\nMy third test revealed a flaw in the agent design. I asked it to extract event data from python.org:\n\n```plaintext\nExtract all event names, locations, dates and URLs of the upcoming events\nin 2026 from https://www.python.org/events/ and save it as a CSV file\n```\n\nInstead of using `extract_data` directly, the agent started looping through `search_web` and `scrape_markdown` calls, seemingly trying to gather context before extraction. It kept going and going.\n\nThe problem: the original agent loop had no iteration limit. If the model keeps requesting tool calls, it runs forever. The fix is adding a `max_iterations` parameter to the entrypoint function:\n\n```python\n@entrypoint()\ndef agent(messages: list, max_iterations: int = 10):\n    \"\"\"ReAct agent loop with iteration limit.\"\"\"\n    llm_response = call_model(messages).result()\n    iterations = 0\n\n    while iterations \u003c max_iterations:\n        if not llm_response.tool_calls:\n            break\n\n        for tc in llm_response.tool_calls:\n            console.print(f\"  [dim]Calling {tc['name']}...[/dim]\")\n\n        tool_futures = [call_tool(tc) for tc in llm_response.tool_calls]\n        tool_results = [f.result() for f in tool_futures]\n\n        messages = add_messages(messages, [llm_response, *tool_results])\n        llm_response = call_model(messages).result()\n        iterations += 1\n\n    return llm_response\n```\n\nTen iterations is plenty for most tasks. If the agent hits the limit, it returns whatever response it has rather than spinning indefinitely. This is a good default for any ReAct-style agent. So, please update your `main.py` file as well before using it for your own tasks.\n\n## Firecrawl's /agent endpoint: the pre-built alternative\n\nWe just built a web scraping agent from scratch using LangGraph and Firecrawl's individual endpoints. But Firecrawl also offers a higher-level option: the `/agent` endpoint. It wraps search, navigation, and extraction into a single API call that takes a natural language prompt instead of code.\n\nThe difference comes down to control versus convenience. Our custom agent lets us define exactly which tools exist, how they behave, and what the model can do with them. The `/agent` endpoint handles all of that internally. You describe what data you want, optionally provide a schema, and Firecrawl figures out the rest.\n\n### How /agent works\n\nThe endpoint accepts a prompt describing your data needs. URLs are optional. If you don't provide any, the agent searches the web to find relevant pages on its own. It then navigates through those pages, handles JavaScript rendering and dynamic content, and extracts structured data matching your schema.\n\nHere's the same competitive research task from our testing section, done with `/agent`:\n\n```python\nfrom firecrawl import Firecrawl\nfrom pydantic import BaseModel\n\nfc = Firecrawl(api_key=\"fc-your-key\")\n\nclass APIComparison(BaseModel):\n    name: str\n    pros: list[str]\n    cons: list[str]\n    pricing: str\n\nclass ComparisonResult(BaseModel):\n    apis: list[APIComparison]\n\nresult = fc.agent(\n    prompt=\"Compare Firecrawl, ScrapingBee, and Browserless. Get pros, cons, and pricing for each.\",\n    schema=ComparisonResult,\n)\n\nprint(result.data)\n```\n\nNo tool definitions. No agent loop. No message history management. The `/agent` endpoint handles web search, page scraping, and data extraction in one call. The Pydantic schema ensures you get typed output instead of unstructured text.\n\n### When to use which\n\nBuild your own agent when you need:\n\n- Custom tools beyond web scraping (database queries, file operations, API calls)\n- Fine-grained control over the reasoning process\n- Integration with existing LangChain/LangGraph workflows\n- Multi-turn conversations with persistent state\n\nUse the `/agent` endpoint when you need:\n\n- Quick data extraction without writing agent code\n- One-off research tasks where setup time matters more than flexibility\n- Structured output from websites you haven't seen before\n- A starting point before deciding if a custom agent is worth building\n\nThe `/agent` endpoint runs on Firecrawl's infrastructure, so you're paying for compute time rather than managing your own LLM calls. Check the [agent documentation](https://docs.firecrawl.dev/features/agent) for current pricing. For high-volume or latency-sensitive applications, a custom agent with your own model deployment might make more sense.\n\nBoth approaches solve the same core problem: getting structured data from websites without brittle selectors. The custom agent gives you a framework to build on. The `/agent` endpoint gives you results without the framework.\n\nP.S: Anthropic has been killing it with their shipping velocity lately. On February 5, 2026, Anthropic released Claude Opus 4.6, a major upgrade to their smartest model. The major update was Agent teams (research preview) using which one can spin up multiple Claude Code agents that work in parallel. We wrote a detailed guide on [Building Apps with Claude Opus 4.6 Agent Teams \u0026 Firecrawl Agent](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/claude-opus-4-6-agent-teams-firecrawl).\n\n## Conclusion\n\nThe finished agent comes in under 300 lines of Python. LangGraph's `@task` and `@entrypoint` decorators handle the reasoning loop, while Firecrawl's SDK wraps scraping, screenshots, extraction, search, and crawling into simple function calls. Rich handles the terminal formatting.\n\nWhat makes this useful is tool composition. You define capabilities once, and the model figures out how to combine them for each request.\n\nA few directions to take this further:\n\n- Add memory persistence so conversations survive restarts\n- Integrate with databases to store extracted data\n- Build a web UI instead of a terminal interface\n- Connect more tools: email, Slack notifications, calendar integration\n- If you use Claude Code, [turn Firecrawl into a custom skill](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/claude-code-skill) for on-demand scraping in your terminal\n\nThe [code is on GitHub](https://github.com/BexTuychiev/firecrawl-scraping-agent). Fork it, swap in your own tools, and build something.\n"])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"a1:Ta92a,"])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"\nIf you're working with data, you've probably noticed something: raw data alone doesn't tell you much. You need context, additional details, and connections to turn those basic records into something actually useful. And the problem is getting worse — [over 40% of CRM data goes stale every year](https://generect.com/blog/hubspot-data-enrichment/), and Gartner research shows poor data quality costs organizations an average of [$12.9 million annually](https://www.integrate.io/blog/data-quality-improvement-stats-from-etl/).\n\nThat's where data enrichment comes in. Simply put, it's the process of taking your existing data and making it better by adding relevant information from other sources. Think of it like filling in the blanks on a form. You start with a name and email address, then add job title, company size, location, and suddenly you understand who you're really talking to.\n\nAs one developer building a B2B data integration tool [shared on Hacker News](https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=45561428):\n\n\u003e Integrating business data into a B2B app or AI agent process is a pain. On one side there's web data providers (Clearbit, Apollo, ZoomInfo) then on the other, 150 year old legacy providers based on government data (D\u0026B, Factset, Moody's, etc). You'd be surprised to learn how much manual work is still happening — teams of people just manually researching business entities all day. — mfrye0 on Hacker News\n\nThis guide walks you through everything you need to know about data enrichment: what it actually means, why businesses care about it, and how you can do it yourself. Whether you're trying to understand your customers better, run smarter marketing campaigns, or just make better decisions with your data, this is worth understanding.\n\nHere's what we'll cover:\n\n- The basics of data enrichment and how it works\n- Tools that make the process easier (including AI-powered options like [Firecrawl](https://firecrawl.dev))\n- Practical strategies you can actually implement\n- Real examples from companies doing this well\n- Common problems and how to avoid them\n\nThe good news? Modern tools have made data enrichment much easier than it used to be. You don't need a huge team or massive budget to get started. Whether you're just learning about this or looking to improve what you're already doing, this guide will help you enhance your data quality and get better results for your business.\n\n## What is data enrichment?\n\nData enrichment is the process of enhancing, refining, and augmenting existing data by combining it with relevant information from multiple sources. This process transforms basic data points into comprehensive, actionable insights that drive better business decisions.\n\nRecent research confirms the growing importance of automated enrichment. A [2025 IEEE review](https://doi.org/10.1109/ICICI65870.2025.11069464) of AI and ML in data governance found that poor data quality costs businesses 15% to 25% of annual revenue, and that traditional rule-based validation is no longer adequate for large-scale, dynamic data ecosystems. Meanwhile, researchers at the University of Illinois Chicago demonstrated that [LLM-generated metadata enrichment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.05411) consistently outperforms content-only retrieval baselines, achieving 82.5% precision when enriching enterprise knowledge bases with automated keyword, summary, and intent metadata.\n\n### Understanding data enrichment tools\n\nModern data enrichment tools automate the complex process of collecting and integrating data from diverse sources. These tools typically offer:\n\n- Automated data collection from websites, APIs, and databases\n- Data validation and cleaning capabilities\n- Schema mapping and transformation features\n- Integration with existing business systems\n- Quality assurance and monitoring\n\nFor example, web scraping tools like Firecrawl let you gather structured data from websites automatically using AI. Instead of manually copying and pasting information, you can use features like [Firecrawl's Agent](https://www.firecrawl.dev/agent) to describe what data you need in plain English, and it handles the extraction for you. We'll walk through a complete Firecrawl data enrichment workflow later in this guide.\n\n### Types of data enrichment services\n\nData enrichment services generally fall into six main categories:\n\n![A comprehensive diagram showing the 6 main categories of data enrichment: Demographic, Behavioral, Company, Psychographic, Geographic, and Contact Data, arranged in a visually appealing infographic format](/images/blog/data_enrichment_guide/enrichment_categories.webp)\n\n1. **Demographic**\n\n   - Adding personal information like age, income, education\n   - Enhancing geographic data with location-specific insights\n   - Including professional and career-related information\n\n2. **Behavioral**\n\n   - Incorporating customer interaction history\n   - Adding purchase patterns and preferences\n   - Including digital footprint data like website visits and engagement\n\n3. **Company**\n\n   - Adding firmographic data (company size, revenue, industry)\n   - Including technological stack information\n   - Incorporating business relationships and hierarchies\n\n4. **Psychographic**\n\n   - Adding personality traits and characteristics\n   - Including personal values and attitudes\n   - Incorporating lifestyle choices and interests\n   - Adding motivation and decision-making patterns\n\n5. **Geographic**\n\n   - Location intelligence and spatial analysis\n   - Regional market data and demographics\n   - Geographic business opportunities\n   - Local economic indicators\n   - Competitive landscape by region\n\n6. **Contact Data**\n   - Email addresses and phone numbers\n   - Social media profiles\n   - Professional contact details\n   - Business communication preferences\n   - Contact verification and validation\n\nThese different types of data enrichment services can be combined and integrated to create comprehensive data profiles. For example, combining demographic and behavioral data provides deeper customer insights, while merging company and geographic data enables better B2B market analysis. The key is selecting the right combination of enrichment types based on your specific business needs and objectives.\n\nAs we move into examining B2B data enrichment specifically, we'll see how these various enrichment types come together to support business decision-making and growth strategies.\n\n### B2B data enrichment fundamentals\n\n[B2B data enrichment](https://docs.firecrawl.dev/use-cases/lead-enrichment) focuses specifically on enhancing business-related information to improve lead generation, account-based marketing, and sales processes. Key aspects include:\n\n- Company verification and validation\n- Decision-maker identification and contact information\n- Industry classification and market positioning\n- Technology stack and tool usage data\n- Company financial health indicators (e.g., revenue ranges $1-10M, $10-50M, etc.)\n- Organizational structure and hierarchy details\n- Employee count brackets (e.g., 1-50, 51-200, 201-1000)\n\nThe effectiveness of B2B data enrichment relies heavily on:\n\n- Data accuracy and freshness through diverse data sources\n- Real-time enrichment for time-sensitive data vs. batch processing for static data\n- Compliance with data protection regulations\n- Integration with existing CRM and sales tools\n- Scalability of enrichment processes\n- Cost-effectiveness and validation across multiple data providers\n\nThese fundamentals form the foundation for successful B2B data enrichment initiatives. When implemented effectively, they enable organizations to make more informed business decisions and develop targeted strategies for growth.\n\nLet's explore the various tools and services available for data enrichment and how to select the right ones for your needs.\n\n## Data enrichment tools and services\n\nData enrichment tools come in various forms, each serving specific use cases and industries. Here are some of the most effective solutions:\n\n| Tool                                                     | Category                         | Description                                                                                                                                | Pricing        |\n| -------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | -------------- |\n| [Firecrawl](https://www.firecrawl.dev/agent)             | Web scraping and data collection | AI-powered web data extraction with natural language prompts. Extract structured data from websites using the Agent endpoint.              | $0-100/mo      |\n| [Bright Data](https://brightdata.com)                    | Web scraping and data collection | Enterprise-grade data collection infrastructure with proxy networks                                                                        | $500+/mo       |\n| [Scrapy](https://scrapy.org/)                            | Web scraping and data collection | Open-source Python framework for custom scraping solutions. Features: Custom extraction rules, proxy rotation, scheduling                  | Free           |\n| [ZoomInfo](https://www.zoominfo.com/)                    | Business intelligence platforms  | Company and contact information enrichment. Integrates with: [Salesforce](https://salesforce.com), [HubSpot](https://hubspot.com), Marketo | $15k+/year     |\n| [Clearbit](https://clearbit.com/)                        | Business intelligence platforms  | Real-time company and person data API. Unique feature: Logo and domain matching                                                            | $99-999/mo     |\n| [InsideView](https://www.demandbase.com/)                | Business intelligence platforms  | Market intelligence and company insights. Specializes in: Sales intelligence and prospecting                                               | $500+/mo       |\n| [Segment](https://segment.com/)                          | Customer data platforms (CDPs)   | Customer data collection and integration. Key differentiator: 300+ pre-built integrations                                                  | $120+/mo       |\n| [Tealium](https://www.tealium.com)                       | Customer data platforms (CDPs)   | Real-time customer data orchestration. Unique feature: Machine learning predictions                                                        | Custom pricing |\n| [mParticle](https://www.mparticle.com/)                  | Customer data platforms (CDPs)   | Customer data infrastructure. Best for: Mobile app analytics                                                                               | $500+/mo       |\n| [Melissa Data](https://www.melissa.com/)                 | Data validation and cleansing    | Address verification and standardization                                                                                                   | $500+/mo       |\n| [DemandTools](https://www.validity.com/demandtools/)     | Data validation and cleansing    | Salesforce data cleansing                                                                                                                  | $999+/year     |\n| [Informatica Data Quality](https://www.informatica.com/) | Data validation and cleansing    | Enterprise data quality management                                                                                                         | Custom pricing |\n\n_Disclaimer: The prices of mentioned tools may vary based on your location and date of visit._\n\nThese tools provide a foundation for data enrichment, but selecting the right service requires careful evaluation of several key factors.\n\n### Choosing the right data enrichment solution\n\nWhen selecting a data enrichment service, organizations must evaluate several critical factors. Data quality metrics serve as the foundation, with accuracy rates ideally exceeding 95% and response times under 200ms. Services should maintain 99.9% uptime SLAs and provide comprehensive data validation methods.\n\nIntegration capabilities determine implementation success. Services should offer well-documented REST APIs, support JSON/CSV formats, and connect with major CRM platforms. The ability to handle both real-time and batch processing (up to 100k records/hour) provides essential flexibility.\n\nCost evaluation requires examining API pricing tiers, volume discounts, and hidden fees. Consider vendor reputation, support quality (24/7 availability), and compliance certifications (GDPR, CCPA, SOC 2). Regular security audits and data handling practices should meet industry standards.\n\nA [Hacker News discussion on real-time enrichment APIs](https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=46034189) highlights common pain points practitioners face when evaluating tools: outdated data in existing platforms, aggressive annual pricing commitments, low data recall resulting in mostly null fields, and privacy concerns around dubious data sources. One commenter raised a key scalability concern:\n\n\u003e If this is truly real-time and not a cached graph, how do you handle rate limiting and CAPTCHAs at scale? Even with public data, on-demand scraping usually requires massive residential proxy rotation which eats that $0.03 margin alive. — warrior44 on Hacker News\n\nThese trade-offs are worth weighing when choosing between cached-database providers and live web extraction approaches like Firecrawl.\n\n### Custom data enrichment solutions\n\nCustom data enrichment solutions provide organizations with tailored approaches to data enhancement. Web scraping platforms like Firecrawl and Selenium enable automated extraction of public data, while custom APIs facilitate direct integration with specialized data sources. Python frameworks such as Selenium, BeautifulSoup and Pandas streamline data processing and transformation workflows. Common pitfalls include rate limiting issues, unstable selectors, and data quality inconsistencies.\n\nThese solutions often incorporate modular architectures for flexibility. Organizations can combine multiple data sources, implement custom validation rules, and design specific enrichment pipelines. Advanced features include proxy rotation for reliable scraping, rate limiting for API compliance, and parallel processing for improved performance. Key challenges include maintaining data consistency across sources and handling API deprecation gracefully.\n\nLLMs are making custom pipelines significantly more accessible. Research on [Prompt2DAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.13487) (Alidu et al., 2024) showed that a hybrid LLM approach can transform natural language descriptions into executable data enrichment workflows with a 78.5% success rate — over twice as efficient as direct prompting per successful pipeline generated. Similarly, [AutoDW](https://doi.org/10.1145/3691620.3695267) (Liu et al., 2024) demonstrated an end-to-end automated data wrangling solution using LLMs that generates transparent, reproducible source code for the entire preparation process, reducing the manual effort that traditionally dominates data enrichment projects.\n\nDevelopment of custom solutions requires careful consideration of scalability and maintenance. Teams should implement robust error handling, comprehensive logging, and automated testing. Documentation and version control ensure long-term sustainability, while modular design enables iterative improvements and feature additions. Consider trade-offs between custom development costs versus off-the-shelf solutions - custom solutions typically require 2-3x more initial investment but offer greater control and flexibility.\n\n### Firecrawl as a custom data enrichment solution\n\nFirecrawl offers powerful AI-powered web extraction capabilities that extend well beyond basic data extraction. Here are some key features that make it an effective data enrichment solution:\n\n1. **Natural language data extraction**\n\nRather than relying on brittle HTML selectors or XPath expressions, Firecrawl allows you to describe the data you want to extract in plain English.\n\n```python\nprompt=\"Find YC W24 dev tool companies and get their contact info and team size\"\n```\n\nThis approach works across different website layouts and remains stable even when sites update their structure. For example, you can request \"find all pricing tables that show enterprise plan features\" or \"extract author biographies from the bottom of articles\" without specifying exact HTML locations.\n\n[Firecrawl /agent](https://www.firecrawl.dev/agent) searches, navigates, and gathers even the most complex websites, finding data in hard-to-reach places and discovering information anywhere on the internet.\n\nIt accomplishes in a few minutes what would take a human many hours. /agent finds and extracts your data, wherever it lives on the web.\n\nThese capabilities are also available through the `scrape_url` method, which is covered in detail in [our guide on using the scrape endpoint](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/mastering-firecrawl-scrape-endpoint).\n\n2. **Recursive website crawling**\n\nBeyond scraping individual URLs, Firecrawl can automatically discover and process entire website sections. The crawler understands site structure semantically, following relevant links while avoiding navigation menus, footers, and other non-content areas. This is especially valuable when enriching data from documentation sites, knowledge bases, or product catalogs. Learn more about the crawling process in [our crawl endpoint guide](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/mastering-the-crawl-endpoint-in-firecrawl).\n\n3. **Multiple output formats**\n\nThe same content can be extracted in different formats to suit your needs:\n\n- Structured data for database storage (as shown in our Amazon example)\n- Markdown for documentation\n- Plain text for natural language processing\n- HTML for web archives\n- Screenshots for visual records\n\nThis flexibility eliminates the need for additional conversion steps in your data pipeline.\n\n4. **Intelligent content processing**\n\nThe AI-powered approach helps solve common web scraping challenges:\n\n- Automatically detecting and extracting data from tables, lists, and other structured elements\n- Understanding content relationships and hierarchies\n- Handling dynamic JavaScript-rendered content\n- Maintaining context across multiple pages\n- Filtering out irrelevant content like ads and popups\n\n5. **Integration capabilities**\n\nFirecrawl offers many [integrations](http://firecrawl.dev/integrations) which means the extracted data can feed directly into modern data and AI workflows:\n\n- Vector databases for [semantic search](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/best-semantic-search-apis)\n- Large language models for analysis\n- Business intelligence tools for reporting\n- Machine learning pipelines for training data\n- Monitoring systems for change detection\n\nThe key advantage across all these use cases is reduced maintenance overhead. Traditional scrapers require constant updates as websites change, while semantic extraction remains stable. This makes it particularly suitable for long-running data enrichment projects where reliability and consistency are crucial.\n\n6. **Parallel processing with /agent for data enrichment at scale**\n\nOne of the biggest bottlenecks in data enrichment is processing speed. Enriching a dataset of 500 companies one query at a time can stretch into hours. Firecrawl's [Parallel Agents](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/introducing-parallel-agents) solve this by letting you batch hundreds or thousands of `/agent` queries simultaneously.\n\nParallel Agents use an intelligent waterfall system. Each query first tries instant retrieval via Spark-1 Fast (at a predictable 10 credits per cell), and only upgrades to full Spark-1 Mini agent research when the fast path can't find your data. This means you get the speed of a cached lookup for common data points (company size, industry, funding stage) and the depth of full web research only when you actually need it.\n\nHere's an example of enriching a batch of companies using the `/agent` endpoint:\n\n```python\nfrom firecrawl import Firecrawl\nfrom pydantic import BaseModel, Field\nfrom typing import List, Optional\n\napp = Firecrawl(api_key=\"fc-YOUR_API_KEY\")\n\nclass CompanyEnrichment(BaseModel):\n    company_name: str = Field(description=\"Official company name\")\n    industry: str = Field(description=\"Primary industry\")\n    employee_count: Optional[str] = Field(None, description=\"Approximate employee count range\")\n    funding_stage: Optional[str] = Field(None, description=\"Latest funding stage (Seed, Series A, etc.)\")\n    headquarters: Optional[str] = Field(None, description=\"HQ city and country\")\n    description: str = Field(description=\"One-sentence company description\")\n\n# Enrich a single company with /agent - no URL needed\nresult = app.agent(\n    prompt=\"Find company details for Notion including their industry, employee count, latest funding stage, headquarters, and a one-sentence description\",\n    schema=CompanyEnrichment,\n    model=\"spark-1-mini\"\n)\n\nprint(result.data)\n```\n\nFor large-scale enrichment, use the [Agent Playground](https://www.firecrawl.dev/app/agent) to upload a CSV of company names and define the fields you want populated. Parallel Agents will process every row simultaneously, streaming results in real time. Green cells indicate successful enrichment, yellow means in-progress, and red flags failures. No workflow configuration required — write one prompt, hit run, and watch your dataset fill in.\n\nThis approach is particularly powerful for use cases like:\n\n- **Firmographic enrichment**: Upload a list of company names, get funding stages, employee counts, and contact information populated in bulk\n- **Competitive research**: Compare features, pricing, and positioning across hundreds of competitors in a single batch\n- **Product catalog extraction**: Pull specs, pricing, and reviews from e-commerce sites at scale, with /agent handling pagination and product page navigation automatically\n\n7. **Fire Enrich: open-source AI data enrichment**\n\nFor teams that want full control over their enrichment pipeline, Firecrawl also offers [Fire Enrich](https://github.com/firecrawl/fire-enrich), an open-source data enrichment tool. Upload a CSV of email addresses, select the fields you want (company description, industry, employee count, funding stage), and Fire Enrich populates your table using specialized AI agents — a Company Research Agent, a Fundraising Intelligence Agent, a People \u0026 Leadership Agent, and a Product \u0026 Technology Agent.\n\nUnlike services that rely on static databases, Fire Enrich researches each company fresh, providing source URLs for every data point. When self-hosted, the only cost is API usage (typically $0.01-0.05 per enrichment), making it a practical alternative to platforms like Clay ($149/month) or Apollo ($59/user/month) for teams with moderate enrichment needs.\n\nFor a complete example of data enrichment process in Firecrawl, you can read a later section on [Custom Data Enrichment in Python: Amazon Data Case Study](#custom-data-enrichment-in-python-amazon-data-case-study).\n\n### Product data enrichment solutions\n\nProduct data enrichment solutions transform basic product information into comprehensive, market-ready datasets. These tools excel at automated attribute extraction, leveraging advanced technologies to identify and categorize product features like dimensions, materials, and industry certifications. Image recognition capabilities enhance product listings with accurate visual data, while competitive pricing analysis ensures market alignment with 95%+ accuracy rates.\n\nModern product enrichment platforms support bulk processing across multiple languages at speeds of 50,000+ products per hour, making them ideal for global operations. They often incorporate industry-specific taxonomies to maintain standardization and enable rich media enhancement for improved product presentation. Key integration capabilities include seamless connections with [Shopify](https://shopify.com), [WooCommerce](https://woocommerce.com), and other major e-commerce platforms, along with built-in data validation methods for ensuring attribute accuracy and completeness.\n\n### Customer data enrichment solutions\n\nCustomer data enrichment platforms serve as central hubs for creating comprehensive customer profiles, achieving match rates of 85-95% for B2B data and 70-85% for B2C data. At their core, these platforms excel at identity resolution, connecting disparate data points to form unified customer views. They incorporate behavioral analytics to understand customer actions and automatically append demographic information to existing profiles, with leading platforms like Clearbit and ZoomInfo offering 98%+ accuracy rates.\n\nIntegration features form the backbone of these platforms, with real-time API access enabling immediate data updates at speeds of 10-20ms per record. Webhook support facilitates automated workflows, while custom field mapping ensures compatibility with existing systems. Sophisticated data synchronization maintains consistency across all connected platforms, with enterprise solutions like [FullContact](https://fullcontact.com) processing up to 1M records per day.\n\nSecurity and compliance remain paramount in customer data enrichment. Modern platforms incorporate robust GDPR compliance measures and granular data privacy controls, typically costing $0.05-0.15 per enriched record at scale. They maintain detailed audit trails and provide comprehensive consent management systems to protect both organizations and their customers.\n\nThe key to successful data enrichment lies in selecting tools that align with your specific use cases while maintaining data quality and compliance standards. Best practices include refreshing enriched data every 3-6 months and implementing data quality monitoring. Organizations should start with a pilot program using one or two tools before scaling to a full implementation, with typical ROI ranging from 3-5x for marketing use cases and 5-8x for sales applications.\n\nOn the research front, Tencent's [SiriusBI system](https://arxiv.org/abs/2411.06102) (Jiang et al., 2025) — deployed across finance, advertising, and cloud divisions — demonstrates what's possible when enrichment is integrated into business intelligence workflows. Their knowledge management module, which uses hybrid storage to enrich SQL generation with business context, achieves 93-97% accuracy across divisions and reduced average task completion time by 30.2%.\n\n## B2B data enrichment: A complete guide\n\nB2B data enrichment has become increasingly critical for organizations seeking to enhance their business intelligence and decision-making capabilities. The shift is visible across practitioner communities: on [r/b2bmarketing](https://www.reddit.com/r/b2bmarketing/comments/1noc9zo/lets_compare_notes_the_b2b_marketing_stack_thats/), sales teams report that fewer tools with deeper integrations outperform sprawling stacks, and that actionable data enrichment makes the real difference in pipeline quality.\n\nOne major trend reshaping B2B enrichment in 2025-2026 is **waterfall enrichment** — querying multiple data providers in sequence until finding the desired information. According to a [2026 comparison of B2B enrichment providers](https://medium.com/@lloyd.rayner/a-comparison-of-b2b-enrichment-providers-2026-60f1e26b9f15), multi-source platforms deliver 30-40 points higher match rates than single providers, pushing coverage from 50-60% to 85-95%.\n\nA [Hacker News discussion on GTM data tools](https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=44501076) surfaced an important nuance about how enrichment tools should be used:\n\n\u003e Tools like Clay and Apollo are often misused for spammy cold outreach — which rarely works. The real value lies in enriching leads who've already shown interest, helping align marketing efforts with the right prospects. — constantinum on Hacker News\n\nLet's explore the key tools and implementation approaches.\n\n### B2B data enrichment tools\n\nEnterprise solutions form the backbone of large-scale B2B data operations, offering comprehensive coverage and high accuracy. These platforms excel at providing deep company insights and market intelligence:\n\n1. Enterprise solutions\n\n- [ZoomInfo](https://zoominfo.com): Industry leader with 95%+ accuracy for company data\n- [D\u0026B Hoovers](https://dnb.com): Comprehensive business intelligence with global coverage\n- [InsideView](https://insideview.com): Real-time company insights and market intelligence\n\nAPI-first platforms enable seamless integration into existing workflows, providing real-time data enrichment capabilities for automated systems:\n\n2. API-First platforms\n\n- [Clearbit enrichment API](https://clearbit.com): Real-time company data lookup\n- [FullContact API](https://docs.fullcontact.com/): Professional contact verification\n- [Hunter.io](https://hunter.io): Email verification and discovery\n\nData validation tools ensure data quality and compliance, critical for maintaining accurate business records:\n\n3. Data Validation Tools\n\n- [Melissa Data](https://melissa.com): Address and contact verification\n- [Neverbounce](https://neverbounce.com): Email validation services\n- [DueDil](https://duedill.com): Company verification and compliance\n\nTypical pricing models range from:\n\n- Pay-per-lookup: $0.05-0.25 per record\n- Monthly subscriptions: $500-5000/month\n- Enterprise contracts: $50,000+ annually\n\n### Best practices for B2B data\n\nCommunity discussions on [r/coldemail](https://www.reddit.com/r/coldemail/comments/1qxgsc5/people_doing_cold_email_for_b2b_what_tools_are/) consistently emphasize that accuracy in 2026 isn't about relying on a single tool — it's about stacking signals from multiple providers and verifying data at each step. A common practitioner stack combines Apollo or ZoomInfo for scale, Clay for enrichment orchestration, and dedicated verification tools for email validation.\n\n1. Data quality management\n\nRegular validation and monitoring are essential for maintaining high-quality B2B data. Implement email format validation using standard regex patterns and schedule bi-monthly data refreshes. Track match rates with a target of \u003e85% accuracy through systematic sampling. As practitioners in the [HubSpot community](https://community.hubspot.com/t5/128172-RevOps-Discussions/Best-data-enrichment-tool-right-now/td-p/1206618) point out, many enrichment platforms give you the same stale company records — real-time enrichment from the actual web (rather than cached databases) is increasingly what teams need.\n\n2. Integration strategy\n\nBuild a robust integration framework using standardized JSON for API communications. Critical components include:\n\n- Real-time alert systems for failed enrichments\n- Retry logic with exponential backoff\n- Comprehensive request logging\n- Cost monitoring and caching mechanisms\n\n3. Compliance and security\n\nEnsure compliance with data protection regulations through proper documentation and security measures. Implement TLS 1.3 for data transfer and AES-256 for storage, while maintaining regular security audits and access reviews.\n\n4. Data Scalability\n\nDesign systems to handle large-scale data processing efficiently. Focus on data partitioning for datasets exceeding 1M records and implement auto-scaling capabilities to manage processing spikes. Monitor key performance metrics to maintain system health.\n\n5. ROI Measurement\n\nTrack the business impact of your enrichment efforts by measuring:\n\n- Cost vs. revenue impact\n- Conversion rate improvements\n- Lead qualification efficiency\n- Sales cycle optimization\n\nKey Performance Indicators:\n\n- Match rate: \u003e85%\n- Data accuracy: \u003e95%\n- Enrichment coverage: \u003e90%\n- Time to value: \\\u003c48 hours\n\nLet's put these best practices into action by exploring a real-world example of data enrichment.\n\n## Custom data enrichment in Python: Amazon data case study\n\nLet's explore a practical example of data enrichment using Python with [Amazon listings data from 2020](https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/promptcloud/amazon-product-dataset-2020). This Kaggle-hosted dataset provides an excellent opportunity to demonstrate how to enrich outdated product information.\n\n### Exploring the Amazon listings dataset\n\nFirst, we should download the dataset stored as a ZIP file on Kaggle servers. These commands pull that zip file and extract the CSV file inside in your current working directory:\n\n```bash\n# Download the zip file\ncurl -L -o amazon-product-dataset-2020.zip\\\n  https://www.kaggle.com/api/v1/datasets/download/promptcloud/amazon-product-dataset-2020\nunzip amazon-product-dataset-2020.zip\n\n# Move the desired file and rename it\nmv home/sdf/marketing_sample_for_amazon_com-ecommerce__20200101_20200131__10k_data.csv amazon_listings.csv\n\n# Delete unnecessary files\nrm -rf home amazon-product-dataset-2020.zip\n```\n\nNext, let's install a few dependencies we are going to use:\n\n```bash\npip install pandas firecrawl-py pydantic python-dotenv\n```\n\nNow, we can load the dataset with Pandas to explore it:\n\n```python\nimport pandas as pd\n\ndf = pd.read_csv(\"amazon_listings.csv\")\n\ndf.shape\n```\n\n```out\n(10002, 28)\n```\n\nThere are 10k products with 28 attributes:\n\n```python\ndf.columns\n```\n\n```out\nIndex(\n    [\n       'Uniq Id', 'Product Name', 'Brand Name', 'Asin', 'Category',\n       'Upc Ean Code', 'List Price', 'Selling Price', 'Quantity',\n       'Model Number', 'About Product', 'Product Specification',\n       'Technical Details', 'Shipping Weight', 'Product Dimensions', 'Image',\n       'Variants', 'Sku', 'Product Url', 'Stock', 'Product Details',\n       'Dimensions', 'Color', 'Ingredients', 'Direction To Use',\n       'Is Amazon Seller', 'Size Quantity Variant', 'Product Description'\n    ], dtype='object'\n)\n```\n\nFor ecommerce listing datasets, missing values are the main problem. Let's see if that's true of this one:\n\n```python\nnull_percentages = df.isnull().sum() / df.shape[0]\n\nnull_percentages.sort_values(ascending=False)\n```\n\n```out\nProduct Description      1.000000\nSku                      1.000000\nBrand Name               1.000000\nAsin                     1.000000\nSize Quantity Variant    1.000000\nList Price               1.000000\nDirection To Use         1.000000\nQuantity                 1.000000\nIngredients              1.000000\nColor                    1.000000\nDimensions               1.000000\nProduct Details          1.000000\nStock                    1.000000\nUpc Ean Code             0.996601\nProduct Dimensions       0.952110\nVariants                 0.752250\nModel Number             0.177165\nProduct Specification    0.163167\nShipping Weight          0.113777\nCategory                 0.082983\nTechnical Details        0.078984\nAbout Product            0.027295\nSelling Price            0.010698\nImage                    0.000000\nProduct Name             0.000000\nProduct Url              0.000000\nIs Amazon Seller         0.000000\nUniq Id                  0.000000\ndtype: float64\n```\n\nBased on the output, we can see that almost 15 attributes are missing for all products while some have partially incomplete data. Only five attributes are fully present:\n\n- Unique ID\n- Is Amazon Seller\n- Product URL\n- Product Name\n- Image\n\nEven when details are present, we can't count on them since they were recorded in 2020 and have probably changed. So, our goal is to enrich this data with updated information as well as filling in the missing columns as best as possible.\n\n### Enriching Amazon listings data with Firecrawl\n\nNow that we've explored the dataset and identified missing information, let's use Firecrawl to enrich our Amazon product data.
