The Firecrawl skill is also available through [OpenClaw's ClawHub ecosystem](/blog/openclaw-firecrawl-guide) for self-hosted personal agents.\n\nOnce you've mastered Claude Code Skills, you should explore [Claude Code plugins](https://claude.com/blog/claude-code-plugins), it has unlocked a new dimension in how developers build and streamline tasks.\n\n*P.S: Check out how we built a [Claude Skills generator](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/claude-skills-generator) with Firecrawl's Agent endpoint.*\n"])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"a4:T7bcd,"])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"\nOpen source web crawlers were already plentiful before the LLM boom. Python developers had Scrapy. Go projects used Colly. Browser automation meant Puppeteer or Playwright. The ecosystem was mature, well-documented, and worked reliably at scale.\n\nThen LLMs changed what people needed from crawlers. Suddenly markdown output mattered more than JSON. Token efficiency became a feature worth optimizing for. New tools launched to handle these requirements, while the old ones stayed focused on what they'd always done well.\n\nNow you're choosing between frameworks built for different eras of web scraping. The classics offer stability but require extra work to feed content into AI pipelines. The newer options output LLM-ready formats but haven't been stress-tested across millions of production crawls.\n\nBelow: 10 crawlers, what they actually do well, and where they break down.\n\n## TL;DR\n\nShort on time? Here's how the 10 crawlers stack up:\n\n| Tool          | Language                    | Best For                                                                | GitHub Stars   | LLM-Ready |\n| ------------- | --------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------- | --------- |\n| Firecrawl     | Python, Node.js, Go, Rust   | All-in-one LLM workflows: markdown, JS rendering, structured extraction, [fast CLI crawling](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/introducing-firecrawl-skill-and-cli)  | 70k+           | Yes       |\n| Crawl4AI      | Python                      | Local LLM integration, RAG pipelines                                    | 58k+           | Yes       |\n| Scrapy        | Python                      | Large-scale structured extraction                                       | 59k+           | No        |\n| Crawlee       | Node.js, Python             | Anti-blocking, modern JS sites                                          | 20k+           | No        |\n| Colly         | Go                          | Fast concurrent crawling                                                | 25k+           | No        |\n| Playwright    | Python, Node.js, Java, .NET | JavaScript-heavy sites, testing                                         | 72k+           | No        |\n| Puppeteer     | Node.js                     | Chrome automation, screenshots                                          | 90k+           | No        |\n| ScrapeGraphAI | Python                      | Schema-based AI extraction                                              | 20k+           | Yes       |\n| Katana        | Go                          | Security research, fast CLI crawling                                    | 14k+           | No        |\n| StormCrawler  | Java                        | Enterprise real-time crawling                                           | Apache Project | No        |\n\n## What is an open-source web crawler?\n\nAn open-source [web crawler](https://www.firecrawl.dev/glossary/web-crawling-apis/how-does-a-web-crawler-work) is software that navigates websites automatically, following links from page to page to discover and download content. The source code is publicly available under licenses like MIT, Apache, or BSD. You can read it, modify it, and run it on your own servers without paying per request or dealing with vendor restrictions.\n\nThe trade-off isn't complicated. Open-source means you control everything:\n\n- **Open-source**: You're running the infrastructure. Scaling, bug fixes, updates - all yours. No monthly bills for [crawl API](https://www.firecrawl.dev/glossary/web-crawling-apis/what-is-web-crawling-api) calls, but you're paying for servers and spending time on maintenance.\n- **Managed services**: The vendor handles servers, updates, and support. Faster to deploy, easier to scale. You pay per request or a flat subscription, which gets expensive at volume but saves engineering time upfront.\n\nTeams pick open-source when they need specific customizations that APIs don't offer, or when per-request pricing doesn't make sense at their scale. Managed services win when you'd rather ship fast than manage crawling infrastructure.\n\n## 1. Firecrawl\n\n![Firecrawl open source web crawler homepage showing LLM-ready markdown conversion](/images/blog/os-web-crawlers/firecrawl-homepage.webp)\n\n**Language:** Python, Node.js, Go, Rust\n\n**Best for:** LLM pipelines that need entire sites converted to clean markdown\n\nFirecrawl's [crawl endpoint](https://docs.firecrawl.dev/features/crawl) starts at a URL and recursively discovers pages by following links.\n\nYou control how deep it goes with `max_discovery_depth`, which sections to crawl using `include_paths` and `exclude_paths`, and whether to stay within the same domain. Point it at documentation, a blog, or any site structure, and it handles [JavaScript](https://www.firecrawl.dev/glossary/web-crawling-apis/what-is-javascript-enabled-crawling) rendering, follows pagination automatically, and converts everything to markdown while stripping navigation and ads.\n\n```python\nfrom firecrawl import Firecrawl\nfrom firecrawl.v2.types import ScrapeOptions\n\napp = Firecrawl()  # Loads API key from FIRECRAWL_API_KEY env var\n\n# Crawl Stripe API docs: only /api and /docs paths, skip blog\nresult = app.crawl(\n    url=\"https://docs.stripe.com/api\",\n    limit=50,\n    max_discovery_depth=3,\n    include_paths=[\"/api/*\", \"/docs/*\"],\n    exclude_paths=[\"/blog/*\"],\n    scrape_options=ScrapeOptions(\n        formats=[\"markdown\", \"links\"],\n        only_main_content=True\n    )\n)\n\n# Each discovered page returns content and all links found\nfor page in result.data:\n    print(f\"Page: {page.metadata.url}\")\n    print(f\"Links found: {len(page.links)}\")\n    print(f\"Content: {page.markdown[:200]}...\")\n```\n\nYou can request multiple formats per page: markdown, HTML, raw HTML, links, or screenshots. The `links` format returns every URL discovered on each page, which is useful when you want to feed link lists into other tools or let users choose what to crawl next.\n\nScrape options like `only_main_content`, `include_tags`, and `exclude_tags` apply to every page the crawler hits, so you get clean extraction across the entire site.\n\nThe markdown output uses roughly 67% fewer tokens than raw HTML, which matters when processing thousands of pages through an LLM.\n\n[Version 2](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/the-worlds-best-web-data-api-v25) introduced natural language prompts. Instead of manually configuring path patterns, describe what you want: \"Extract API documentation and reference guides.\" Firecrawl translates this into include paths, depth settings, and other parameters automatically.\n\nFor rapid URL discovery without full content extraction, the [map endpoint](https://docs.firecrawl.dev/features/map) returns every link on a site in seconds.\n\nThe open-source version runs via Docker and covers core crawling features. The hosted API adds higher concurrency, managed infrastructure, and an extract endpoint that applies LLM-based structured extraction during the crawl.\n\n**Pros:**\n\n- Discovers and follows links with configurable depth and URL pattern matching\n- Handles JavaScript rendering and pagination automatically\n- Multiple output formats per page (markdown, HTML, links, screenshots)\n- Natural language crawl prompts replace manual parameter configuration (v2)\n- Map endpoint for rapid site structure analysis without full scraping\n- LLM-powered structured extraction during crawl\n- SDKs for Python, Node.js, Go, Rust with LangChain/LlamaIndex integrations\n- Respects robots.txt and handles rate limiting\n\n**Cons:**\n\n- Self-hosted version lacks features available in the paid API\n- Pricing scales with crawl volume\n- Requires Docker for local deployment\n\n**What users say:**\n\n\u003e Firecrawl has improved the efficiency of my data scraping tasks and saved me a lot of time by eliminating complicated setup. - [SourceForge review](https://sourceforge.net/software/product/Firecrawl/)\n\n\u003e Some teams have benchmarked Firecrawl 50x faster than competitors in real-world agent tasks. - [Dev.to comparison](https://dev.to/apify/firecrawl-vs-apify-2025-guide-for-ai-and-data-teams-42e3)\n\n\u003cdiv style={{ maxWidth: 550, margin: \"0 auto\" }}\u003e\n  \u003ciframe\n    src=\"https://platform.twitter.com/embed/Tweet.html?id=1780299595484131836\u0026lang=en\u0026theme=light\u0026dnt=true\"\n    style={{ width: \"100%\", height: \"clamp(420px, 70vh, 900px)\", border: 0 }}\n    loading=\"lazy\"\n    title=\"Embedded Tweet\"\n  /\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\n**Pricing:** Completely free and open source under AGPL license. Hosted plans start at -\u0026#36;16/month (3,000 credits) and scale to -\u0026#36;333/month (500,000 credits), with a free tier offering 500 one-time credits.\n\n## 2. Crawl4AI\n\n![Crawl4AI Python web crawler homepage for LLM and RAG pipelines](/images/blog/os-web-crawlers/crawl4ai-homepage.webp)\n\n**Language:** Python  \n**Best for:** RAG pipelines and AI agents that need local LLM support without API costs\n\nCrawl4AI launched in mid-2024 as a Python-native approach to LLM-ready crawling, built for developers who want full control over their infrastructure. The project took off fast, hitting #1 on GitHub's trending page and amassing over 58,000 stars in under a year.\n\nIts pitch is straightforward: a local-first crawler that outputs clean markdown without requiring external API calls, making it popular for teams building RAG pipelines or autonomous agents on their own servers.\n\nThe library converts web pages into markdown optimized for LLMs. For query-specific extraction, you can apply a BM25 content filter that ranks and keeps only sections relevant to your search terms. You can plug in local models through Ollama or connect to external APIs like OpenAI or Deepseek. This flexibility appeals to teams that care about data sovereignty or want predictable costs at scale.\n\nRecent versions added \"Adaptive Web Crawling\" where the crawler learns reliable selectors over time, plus webhook infrastructure for job queues in Docker deployments. Playwright handles JavaScript rendering under the hood by default.\n\n**Pros:**\n\n- Fully offline operation with local LLMs (no API costs)\n- Optional BM25 filtering for query-focused extraction\n- Apache License allows commercial use and modifications\n- Learns and adapts selectors across crawling sessions\n- Active community with frequent releases\n\n**Cons:**\n\n- Extraction quality depends on your choice of LLM\n- Steeper learning curve than API-based tools\n- \"Free\" still means paying for LLM inference or self-hosting infrastructure\n\n**Pricing:** Free and open source under Apache License 2.0. Infrastructure and LLM costs are yours to manage.\n\n## 3. Scrapy\n\n![Scrapy Python web scraping framework homepage](/images/blog/os-web-crawlers/scrapy-homepage.webp)\n\n**Language:** Python  \n**Best for:** Large-scale structured data extraction from static websites\n\nScrapy has been the [default choice for serious Python web scraping](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/list-crawling-python-beautifulsoup-scrapy-firecrawl) since 2008. Built on Twisted, an event-driven networking engine, it handles thousands of concurrent requests without breaking a sweat. The framework processes HTTP responses at the raw level, skipping browser overhead entirely. For static HTML sites, this makes it [significantly faster than browser-based tools](https://docs.scrapy.org/en/latest/) like Playwright or Puppeteer.\n\nThe architecture splits crawling into distinct components: spiders define what to scrape, middleware handles request/response processing, and item pipelines clean and store your data. This modularity pays off at scale but comes with a learning curve. Expect to spend time understanding how these pieces fit together before you're productive.\n\nWhere Scrapy falls short is JavaScript. It was built for an era of server-rendered HTML, and dynamic content requires bolting on Splash or scrapy-playwright. If your target sites rely heavily on client-side rendering, other tools in this list will save you headaches.\n\n**Pros:**\n\n- Battle-tested performance at scale (handles millions of pages)\n- Extensive middleware ecosystem for proxies, retries, throttling\n- Built-in support for exporting to JSON, CSV, XML\n- Massive community with years of Stack Overflow answers and tutorials\n\n**Cons:**\n\n- No native JavaScript rendering\n- Steep learning curve for the component architecture\n- Overkill for simple scraping tasks\n\n**Pricing:** Free and open source under BSD 3-Clause license.\n\n## 4. Playwright\n\n![Playwright browser automation framework homepage by Microsoft](/images/blog/os-web-crawlers/playwright-homepage.webp)\n\n**Language:** Python, Node.js, Java, .NET  \n**Best for:** JavaScript-heavy sites that need real browser rendering and interaction\n\nPlaywright wasn't built for web scraping. Microsoft designed it as a [testing framework](https://playwright.dev/), and that DNA shows in everything from its API design to its documentation. But testers and scrapers face the same problem: modern websites render content client-side, hide data behind user interactions, and fight automation at every turn.\n\nThe same features that make Playwright good at testing (auto-waiting, network interception, multi-browser support) make it excellent at scraping sites that break simpler tools.\n\nThe library controls Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit through a single API. Puppeteer now supports Firefox via WebDriver BiDi, but Playwright was built from day one for cross-browser work, and that maturity shows. Auto-wait handles timing issues that plague other browser automation tools. Instead of sprinkling sleep statements everywhere, Playwright waits for elements to become actionable before proceeding.\n\nWhere Playwright struggles is scale. Each browser context consumes memory, and running hundreds of concurrent sessions requires serious infrastructure. There's also no built-in crawling logic. You handle pagination, link following, and request queuing yourself, or wrap Playwright in something like Crawlee that handles this for you.\n\nFor single-page extraction or moderate concurrency, these limitations rarely matter. For crawling millions of URLs, you'll want additional tooling.\n\n**Pros:**\n\n- Supports Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit from one codebase\n- Auto-wait eliminates most timing-related flakiness\n- Handles login flows, infinite scroll, and lazy loading natively\n- Network interception lets you block ads, images, or tracking scripts\n- Strong documentation and Microsoft backing\n\n**Cons:**\n\n- No built-in crawling (pagination and queuing are manual)\n- Memory-intensive at scale\n- Prone to detection without stealth plugins\n- Doesn't solve CAPTCHAs\n\n**Pricing:** Free and open source under Apache License 2.0.\n\n## 5. Puppeteer\n\n![Puppeteer headless Chrome automation library homepage by Google](/images/blog/os-web-crawlers/puppeteer-homepage.webp)\n\n**Language:** Node.js  \n**Best for:** Chrome automation, screenshots, and teams already working in JavaScript\n\nPuppeteer came first.\n\nGoogle released it in 2017 to give developers a proper way to control Chrome programmatically, and for years it was the default choice for headless browser work. Then two of its lead developers left for Microsoft and built Playwright. The libraries share DNA, similar APIs, overlapping features, but Puppeteer stayed focused on Chrome while Playwright expanded to Firefox and WebKit.\n\nThat Chrome-first focus isn't necessarily a weakness. Tighter integration means Puppeteer often handles edge cases in Chrome better than cross-browser tools can. Firefox support landed in v23 via WebDriver BiDi, though Chrome remains the primary target.\n\nThe puppeteer-extra ecosystem adds stealth plugins that patch common bot detection vectors, which matters when you're scraping sites that actively fight automation. If your targets block headless browsers, puppeteer-extra-plugin-stealth is often the first thing experienced scrapers reach for.\n\nThe tradeoffs hit at scale. Each Chrome instance eats memory, and running dozens of concurrent sessions means watching RAM usage climb fast. There's no built-in crawling logic either. You handle pagination, retries, and request queuing yourself or wrap Puppeteer in a framework like Crawlee.\n\nFor smaller jobs or when you need precise control over a single browser session, these limitations don't matter much. For crawling at volume, you'll spend time on infrastructure that other tools handle out of the box.\n\n**Pros:**\n\n- Tighter Chrome integration than cross-browser alternatives\n- Strong stealth plugin ecosystem for anti-detection\n- Automatically installs compatible Chromium, no version management headaches\n- 90k GitHub stars and active Google backing\n- Extensive Stack Overflow coverage (7,000+ questions)\n\n**Cons:**\n\n- Firefox supported but Chrome remains the focus (fewer Firefox-specific features)\n- JavaScript-only, no Python or other language bindings\n- Memory-hungry at scale\n- No built-in crawling, queuing, or retry logic\n\n**Pricing:** Free and open source under Apache License 2.0.\n\n## 6. ScrapeGraphAI\n\n![ScrapeGraphAI LLM-powered web scraping library homepage](/images/blog/os-web-crawlers/scrapegraph-homepage.webp)\n\n**Language:** Python  \n**Best for:** Prototyping scrapers without writing selectors\n\nScrapeGraphAI uses LLM-powered extraction with a pipeline architecture built on directed graphs. The SmartCrawler starts from a URL, follows internal links using breadth-first traversal, and extracts data from each page based on a natural language prompt. You set parameters like `depth=2`, `max_pages=100`, and `same_domain_only=True`, and the crawler handles discovery while the LLM handles parsing.\n\nInstead of writing CSS selectors or XPath, you describe what you want and provide a Pydantic schema. The LLM interprets page structure based on context rather than fixed element paths. When it works, you skip the selector maintenance that breaks traditional scrapers. When it doesn't, debugging gets harder because the LLM's decisions aren't always transparent.\n\nThe tradeoff is cost, speed, and consistency. Every page extraction burns tokens, and LLM inference adds latency. Extraction quality varies depending on page complexity and how well the LLM understands the structure. For large crawls, API costs stack up. You can run local models through Ollama, but that shifts the expense to your own infrastructure.\n\n**Pros:**\n\n- Crawls n-levels deep with configurable page limits and domain restrictions\n- No selectors to write or maintain\n- Pydantic schemas enforce consistent output structure\n- Works with cloud LLMs (OpenAI, Groq, Azure, Gemini) or local models via Ollama\n\n**Cons:**\n\n- Extraction quality varies by page complexity\n- Token costs add up on large crawls\n- Slower than HTTP-only crawlers\n- No native infinite scroll or JavaScript interaction handling\n- Harder to debug when extraction fails (LLM reasoning isn't always clear)\n\n**Pricing:** Free and open source under MIT license. LLM costs depend on your provider.\n\n## 7. StormCrawler\n\n![Apache StormCrawler enterprise web crawler homepage](/images/blog/os-web-crawlers/stormcrawler-homepage.webp)\n\n**Language:** Java  \n**Best for:** Enterprise teams running continuous crawls on existing Storm infrastructure\n\nStormCrawler takes a different approach than everything else on this list. Where most crawlers work in batches (fetch URLs, process, repeat), StormCrawler treats crawling as a stream. URLs flow through a directed acyclic graph of processing components, getting fetched, parsed, and stored continuously rather than in discrete jobs. This architecture makes sense if you're already running Apache Storm for other workloads or if your use case involves URLs arriving over time rather than from a static list.\n\nThe project graduated from Apache Incubator to Top-Level Project status in June 2025, a milestone that signals mature governance and production readiness. Organizations use it for search engine indexing, web archiving, and scenarios where low latency matters more than simplicity. You can run it on a single machine for development or scale across a Storm cluster with the same codebase.\n\nThe SDK approach means StormCrawler gives you components rather than a turnkey solution. Spouts pull URLs from your storage layer (OpenSearch, Solr, or custom). Bolts handle fetching, parsing via Apache Tika, and writing results back. You wire these together into topologies that match your requirements. This flexibility comes at the cost of setup time; expect to spend hours configuring before your first crawl runs.\n\n**Pros:**\n\n- Stream processing handles continuous URL feeds without batch overhead\n- Scales horizontally across Storm clusters\n- Apache Tika integration parses PDFs, Office docs, and other formats\n- OpenSearch and Solr modules included\n- Apache TLP status means long-term project stability\n- Runs identically on single nodes or distributed clusters\n\n**Cons:**\n\n- Requires Apache Storm knowledge (steep learning curve if you're new to it)\n- SDK approach means more assembly than other tools\n- Overkill for simple scraping tasks\n- Smaller community than Python alternatives\n- No built-in anti-bot measures\n\n**Pricing:** Free and open source under Apache License 2.0.\n\n## 8. Katana\n\n![Katana web crawler by ProjectDiscovery for security research](/images/blog/os-web-crawlers/katana-homepage.webp)\n\n**Language:** Go  \n**Best for:** Security researchers and pentesters who need fast URL discovery\n\nKatana comes from ProjectDiscovery, the team behind nuclei and other security-focused tools. It's built for a specific job: crawl a target quickly, [extract](https://www.firecrawl.dev/extract) every URL, endpoint, and JavaScript file, then feed that list into other tools for vulnerability scanning. Where general-purpose crawlers optimize for data extraction, Katana optimizes for reconnaissance.\n\nThe default mode parses raw HTTP responses without rendering JavaScript. Add the `-headless` flag and it spins up a browser that captures XHR requests, dynamically loaded scripts, and other endpoints that static analysis misses. Recent versions improved network request capturing to include XHR, Fetch, and Script resource types, making headless mode more thorough for modern SPAs.\n\nInstallation requires Go 1.24+ or you can pull the official Docker image which bundles Chromium. The CLI interface fits into shell pipelines naturally. Point it at a domain, pipe the output to nuclei or httpx, and you've got a basic recon workflow in one line. For complex setups, YAML config files handle form auto-filling and custom field extraction.\n\nKatana won't replace Scrapy for data extraction or Firecrawl for LLM pipelines. It's a specialist tool for security work, and it does that job well.\n\n**Pros:**\n\n- Extracts endpoints that static crawlers miss (headless mode captures XHR/Fetch)\n- Single binary with no runtime dependencies\n- Plays nicely with other ProjectDiscovery tools (nuclei, httpx, subfinder)\n- Active development with frequent releases throughout 2025\n- Respects scope restrictions to avoid crawling outside target domains\n\n**Cons:**\n\n- No structured data extraction (outputs URLs, not parsed content)\n- Security-focused feature set limits general scraping use cases\n- Headless mode requires Chromium installation\n\n**Pricing:** Free and open source under MIT license.\n\n## 9. Colly\n\n![Colly fast web scraping framework for Go developers](/images/blog/os-web-crawlers/colly-homepage.webp)\n\n**Language:** Go  \n**Best for:** Go developers who want speed without browser overhead\n\nGo scrapers run fast. In benchmarks, they finish in half the time Python takes on identical datasets. Colly leans into this advantage with a callback-based architecture that processes over 1,000 requests per second on a single core. No browser, no JavaScript engine, just HTTP requests and HTML parsing.\n\nThe API centers on [collectors and callbacks](http://go-colly.org/). You create a collector, attach handlers for different events (HTML elements, requests, responses, errors), and call `Visit()`. Colly handles cookies, redirects, rate limiting, and parallel requests behind the scenes. The same patterns that work for scraping a single page work for crawling thousands. Per-domain rate limiting prevents you from hammering servers. Built-in caching avoids redundant requests. Distributed mode spreads work across machines when a single process isn't enough.\n\nThe tradeoff is JavaScript. Colly doesn't render it. If your target site loads content dynamically, you need chromedp or a headless browser. For server-rendered HTML, which still covers most of the web, Colly handles the job faster than anything else on this list.\n\n**Pros:**\n\n- Compiles to a single binary with no runtime dependencies\n- Handles 1k+ requests/second on modest hardware\n- Built-in rate limiting, caching, and robots.txt compliance\n- Clean callback API that scales from scripts to production crawlers\n- 25k GitHub stars and active maintenance (v2.2.0 shipped March 2025)\n\n**Cons:**\n\n- No JavaScript rendering (need chromedp for dynamic sites)\n- Go-only (no Python or Node bindings)\n- Smaller ecosystem than Scrapy\n\n**Pricing:** Free and open source under Apache License 2.0.\n\n## 10. Crawlee\n\n![Crawlee web scraping library with anti-blocking features by Apify](/images/blog/os-web-crawlers/crawlee-homepage.webp)\n\n**Language:** Node.js, Python  \n**Best for:** Production crawlers that need anti-blocking without rolling your own infrastructure\n\nCrawlee grew out of Apify's internal SDK, rebuilt from scratch and launched in August 2022 as a standalone library. The pitch: a unified interface that works the same whether you're making raw HTTP requests, controlling Puppeteer, or running Playwright. You write your crawler once, swap the underlying engine based on what the target site requires, and Crawlee handles the plumbing that makes production scraping painful.\n\nThat plumbing is where Crawlee earns its reputation. Browser fingerprint rotation patches the JavaScript properties that bot detectors check. Request queuing persists to disk, so crashed crawlers resume where they left off. Proxy rotation spreads requests across your pool automatically. Session management ties proxies to browser contexts so sites see consistent \"users\" rather than random IP switches mid-session. These features exist in other tools as plugins or manual implementations. Crawlee ships them as defaults.\n\nThe Python version hit beta in July 2024 and reached v1.0 in September 2025, accumulating over 6,000 GitHub stars. It mirrors the Node.js API closely enough that tutorials for one mostly apply to the other. The Node.js version integrates with Cheerio for parsing, while Python uses BeautifulSoup. Both can run headful or headless depending on whether you need to debug visually.\n\nThe Apify connection cuts both ways. The library works fine standalone, but some features push you toward their platform. Cloud deployment, managed proxies, and the Actor ecosystem all integrate smoothly if you're paying. Self-hosting everything means handling more infrastructure yourself.\n\n**Pros:**\n\n- Unified API across HTTP, Puppeteer, and Playwright backends\n- Fingerprint rotation and session management built in\n- Persistent request queues survive crashes and restarts\n- Available in both Node.js and Python with similar APIs\n- AutoscaledPool adjusts concurrency based on CPU and memory usage\n\n**Cons:**\n\n- Apify platform integration can feel like vendor lock-in\n- Heavier than simpler HTTP libraries for basic tasks\n- Python version still newer with a smaller community than Node.js\n\n**Pricing:** Free and open source under Apache License 2.0. Apify platform pricing starts at -\u0026#36;39/month if you want managed infrastructure.\n\n## How to choose the right web crawling solution\n\nThe first question isn't about features or GitHub stars. It's about output format. If you're feeding pages into an LLM, you want markdown, not raw HTML. Firecrawl, Crawl4AI, and ScrapeGraphAI handle this conversion natively. The rest require you to process HTML yourself.\n\nThe second question is [JavaScript rendering](https://www.firecrawl.dev/glossary/web-crawling-apis/what-is-javascript-enabled-crawling). Modern sites load content dynamically, and a crawler that only parses the initial HTML response will miss it:\n\n- **Browser-based** (render JavaScript): Playwright, Puppeteer, Crawlee\n- **HTTP-only** (faster, static HTML only): Scrapy, Colly, Katana, StormCrawler\n- **LLM-native** (handle rendering internally): Firecrawl, Crawl4AI, ScrapeGraphAI\n\nIf you're going the LLM-native route, Firecrawl and Crawl4AI handle JavaScript internally, so you skip this question entirely.\n\nOnce you've narrowed by output format and rendering needs, language decides the rest. Go developers pick Colly or Katana. Python shops choose between Scrapy and the newer LLM-focused tools. Node.js teams lean toward Crawlee or Puppeteer. Java enterprise environments make StormCrawler viable where it wouldn't be otherwise.\n\nScale matters less than people think. Scrapy and StormCrawler handle millions of pages, but most projects never hit that volume. Browser-based tools work fine for thousands.\n\nThe honest take: there's no universally \"best\" crawler.\n\nIf you're unsure, Firecrawl covers the most ground: LLM-ready markdown, JavaScript rendering, structured extraction during crawl, and SDKs across four languages. Otherwise, pick what fits your stack. You can always switch later.\n\n## Conclusion\n\nMost scraping projects fail because of anti-bot measures or messy HTML, not because someone picked the wrong crawler. The tooling has gotten good enough that language preference and team familiarity matter more than feature checklists. Python teams will reach for Python tools. Go developers will pick Go. That's fine.\n\nWhat's changed is how much the open-source ecosystem now handles for you. JavaScript rendering, proxy rotation, markdown conversion, LLM-friendly output. Problems that used to require custom infrastructure are now a flag or config option. The hard part isn't choosing anymore. It's defining what you actually need to extract.\n\nIf you want to go deeper, also read: [List Crawling: Extract Structured Data From Websites at Scale](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/list-crawling-python-beautifulsoup-scrapy-firecrawl) and [Crawlbench: LLM extraction benchmark](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/crawlbench-llm-extraction).\n\n## Frequently asked questions\n\n### What's the difference between a web crawler and a web scraper?\n\nA crawler discovers URLs by following links across a site. A scraper extracts data from specific pages. Most tools in this list do both, but the emphasis differs. Scrapy and Crawlee are crawl-first with extraction built in. Puppeteer and Playwright are page-focused tools you can wrap in crawling logic. The distinction matters less than it used to since modern frameworks bundle both capabilities.\n\n### Which open-source crawler is best for beginners?\n\nFirecrawl is the most popular open-source web crawler today if you want reliable results fast without much setup.\n\n### Can I use these crawlers for commercial projects?\n\nYes. All ten tools use permissive licenses (Apache 2.0, MIT, BSD, or AGPL). Check the specific license for attribution requirements. Firecrawl's AGPL means you'd need to open-source modifications if you distribute the software, but using the API or running it internally doesn't trigger that clause.\n\n### Which crawler is the fastest?\n\nFor raw HTTP requests, Colly and Scrapy lead the pack. Both handle thousands of requests per second. Browser-based tools (Playwright, Puppeteer) are slower because they render full pages. Speed rarely matters as much as reliability. A crawler that finishes 10x faster but misses half the content isn't faster in any practical sense.\n"])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"a5:T5204,"])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"\nWeb scraping and web crawling get thrown around like they mean the same thing. They don't.\n\nA scraper extracts data from a _specific page_. A crawler navigates across _multiple pages or entire websites_. One targets a single URL, the other maps out a whole site. Knowing when to use which saves you from building the wrong solution.\n\nThis guide breaks down the difference between scrapers and crawlers, explains how each works under the hood, and walks you through two practical projects: a job listing extractor (scraping) and a blog content collector (crawling). Both projects use Firecrawl, so you can run the code yourself.\n\nBy the end, you'll know exactly which approach fits your use case.\n\n## Web scraper vs web crawler: the core difference\n\nHere's how they compare at a glance:\n\n|                      | Web Scraping                          | Web Crawling                     |\n| -------------------- | ------------------------------------- | -------------------------------- |\n| **Scope**            | Single page or specific URLs          | Entire website or multiple sites |\n| **Primary goal**     | Extract specific data                 | Discover and index pages         |\n| **Output**           | Structured data (JSON, CSV, database) | List of URLs or page content     |\n| **Speed**            | Fast (targets known pages)            | Slower (must traverse links)     |\n| **Use case example** | Pull product prices from one page     | Index all products across a site |\n| **Analogy**          | Fishing with a spear                  | Fishing with a net               |\n\n![Web scraping vs web crawling comparison diagram showing scraping as targeted single-page extraction and crawling as multi-page discovery across a website](/images/blog/scraper-vs-crawler/scraper_vs_crawler.webp)\n\nA simple way to remember it: **scraping is about _what's on a page_, while crawling is about _finding pages_.**\n\nA web scraper targets pages you already know about. You give it a URL, tell it what data you want (prices, titles, descriptions), and it extracts that information into a structured format. The scraper doesn't care what other pages exist on the site.\n\nA web crawler, on the other hand, starts with one or more seed URLs and follows links to discover new pages. Search engines like Google use crawlers to find and index the web. The crawler's job is navigation and discovery first, with data collection as a secondary step.\n\nThe confusion between these terms exists because many tools do both, and in practice, the two techniques often work together. When someone says \"I scraped that website,\" they might mean they crawled it first to discover all the product URLs, then scraped each page for specific details.\n\nThink of crawling as the reconnaissance phase and scraping as the extraction phase.\n\n## What is web scraping\n\nA scraper grabs data from pages you already know about. You point it at a URL, tell it what to extract, and it hands you structured data:\n\n1. **Send a request** to the target URL\n2. **Get back HTML** (or wait for JavaScript to render)\n3. **Parse the page** and locate the elements you care about\n4. **Pull the data** into a usable format\n5. **Store it** somewhere (JSON, CSV, database, whatever)\n\nSimple enough until you hit a modern, [dynamic website](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/headless-web-scraping-dynamic-websites).\n\nMost sites today load content through JavaScript, so the HTML you get from a basic request is a skeleton with empty divs. You need a headless browser (Puppeteer, Playwright) to let the page fully render before you can see what's actually there.\n\nParsing is where you spend most of your time. [CSS selectors](https://www.firecrawl.dev/glossary/web-scraping-apis/what-is-css-selector-web-scraping) work for straightforward layouts. XPath handles messier DOM structures. Some newer tools, like Firecrawl, use AI to identify fields without manual selectors (you can use natural language), which saves you from rewriting everything when a site tweaks its layout.\n\nFor a deeper dive into scraping fundamentals, check out this [web scraping intro for beginners](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/web-scraping-intro-for-beginners).\n\n## What is web crawling\n\nThink of a crawler as a scraper that doesn't know where to go yet.\n\nYou give it a starting URL, and it figures out the rest by fetching a page, pulling out every link, adding new ones to a queue, and repeating.\n\nA crawler pointed at a blog's homepage will find links to posts, then links within those posts, then maybe links to author pages or categories. It keeps going until you tell it to stop.\n\n![Web crawler page discovery process showing how a crawler traverses links from a seed URL to discover new pages across a website](/images/blog/scraper-vs-crawler/crawler_discovery.webp)\n\nThat stopping condition matters. Set a limit of 50 pages, a max depth of 3 links from the start, or a URL pattern like \"only follow `/blog/*` paths.\" Without boundaries, you'll crawl forever or accidentally wander off to external sites.\n\nThe order of crawling changes what you get. [Breadth-first](https://www.firecrawl.dev/glossary/web-crawling-apis/what-is-breadth-first-vs-depth-first-crawling) stays shallow and wide, visiting everything on the homepage before going deeper. Depth-first picks one path and follows it all the way down. Most site-mapping jobs want breadth-first. Most \"find that one buried page\" jobs want depth-first.\n\nDeduplication saves you from yourself. Sites love having five URLs that point to the same page (query strings, trailing slashes, tracking parameters). Your crawler needs to recognize these or it'll waste half its time revisiting content.\n\nOne more thing: be polite. Check `robots.txt`, throttle your requests, use a real user agent. Hammering a site with rapid-fire requests gets you blocked and annoys the people running it.\n\n## Web scraping in action: Job listing extractor\n\nTime to put scraping into practice. We'll build a job listing extractor that pulls openings from OpenAI's careers page and saves them to a CSV file.\n\nAt the time of writing this article, OpenAI has over 400 open positions, so we'll apply the \"Applied AI\" filter on their careers page to narrow it down. The filtered URL looks messy (filter tokens get encoded), and these tokens can rotate.\n\nIf you're following along, visit [openai.com/careers/search](https://openai.com/careers/search/), apply your own filters, and copy the URL from your browser.\n\n![OpenAI careers page showing job listings that will be scraped using Firecrawl](/images/blog/scraper-vs-crawler/openai_careers.webp)\n\n### The traditional approach and its problems\n\nA standard scraping workflow would look like this: send a request, get the HTML, find the right CSS selectors for job titles, teams, and locations, then parse everything into a list.\n\nBut OpenAI's careers page is JavaScript-heavy.\n\nA basic HTTP request returns an empty shell because the job listings load dynamically. You'd need a headless browser like Playwright to render the page first.\n\nEven with rendering solved, you're stuck with brittle selectors. The job title might be in a `div.job-title` today and a `span.position-name` next month. Every site redesign breaks your scraper.\n\nWe'll use [Firecrawl](https://www.firecrawl.dev/) to solve both problems.\n\nFirecrawl renders JavaScript automatically and uses LLM-based extraction instead of CSS selectors. The [scrape endpoint](https://docs.firecrawl.dev/features/scrape) handles single-page extraction with built-in JS rendering. You describe what data you want in a schema, and the LLM figures out where to find it on the page. If the site layout changes, the extraction still works because the LLM adapts to new structures.\n\nFirecrawl offers a free tier with 500 credits to start. If you prefer self-hosting, it's also [open source](https://github.com/firecrawl/firecrawl).\n\n### Setup\n\nInstall the dependencies:\n\n```bash\npip install firecrawl python-dotenv pandas pydantic\n```\n\nGet your API key from [firecrawl.dev](https://www.firecrawl.dev/) and add it to a `.env` file:\n\n```bash\nFIRECRAWL_API_KEY=fc-your-api-key-here\n```\n\n### Defining the extraction schema\n\nDefine your target structure with Pydantic:\n\n```python\nfrom firecrawl import FirecrawlApp\nfrom pydantic import BaseModel\nfrom dotenv import load_dotenv\nimport pandas as pd\nimport os\n\nload_dotenv()\n\napp = FirecrawlApp(api_key=os.getenv(\"FIRECRAWL_API_KEY\"))\n\nurl = \"https://openai.com/careers/search/?c=e1e973fe-6f0a-475f-9362...\"\n\n\nclass JobListing(BaseModel):\n    title: str\n    team: str\n    location: str\n    url: str\n\n\nclass JobListings(BaseModel):\n    jobs: list[JobListing]\n```\n\nThe `JobListing` class defines the four fields we want for each job. `JobListings` wraps them in a list since we're extracting multiple items from one page.\n\n### Scraping with structured extraction\n\nOne API call handles both the scraping and the extraction:\n\n```python\nresult = app.v1.scrape_url(\n    url,\n    formats=[\"extract\"],\n    extract={\n        \"prompt\": \"Extract all job listings with title, team, location, and URL.\",\n        \"schema\": JobListings.model_json_schema(),\n    },\n)\n\njobs = result.extract.get(\"jobs\", [])\nprint(f\"Found {len(jobs)} jobs\")\n```\n\n```bash\nFound 62 jobs\n```\n\nThe `formats=[\"extract\"]` tells Firecrawl to run LLM-based extraction. Firecrawl renders the JavaScript, waits for the page to load, and passes the content to an LLM that maps it to your schema.\n\n### Saving to CSV\n\nWith the data in a list of dictionaries, converting to a DataFrame and saving takes two lines:\n\n```python\ndf = pd.DataFrame(jobs)\ndf.to_csv(\"openai_jobs.csv\", index=False)\nprint(df.head(10).to_string(index=False))\n```\n\n```plaintext\n                                            title                   team      location\n     Backend Software Engineer - B2B Applications       B2B Applications   2 locations\n                Backend Software Engineer, Growth Applied AI Engineering San Francisco\n                         Data Engineer, Analytics Applied AI Engineering San Francisco\n                       Engineering Manager, Atlas Applied AI Engineering San Francisco\n              Engineering Manager, ChatGPT Growth Applied AI Engineering San Francisco\n        Engineering Manager, Enterprise Ecosystem Applied AI Engineering       Seattle\n Engineering Manager, Monetization Infrastructure    Engineering Manager San Francisco\n```\n\nSixty-two jobs extracted from a JavaScript-heavy page, parsed into structured data, and saved to disk. No manual HTML parsing, no CSS selectors, no headless browser configuration. That's the scraping workflow: one page, targeted extraction, structured output.\n\nFor more scraping patterns, see [mastering the Firecrawl scrape endpoint](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/mastering-firecrawl-scrape-endpoint).\n\n## Web crawling in action: Blog content collector\n\nNow let's flip the approach. Instead of extracting data from a single page we already know about, we'll discover and collect content from an entire blog. The target: Firecrawl's own blog at `firecrawl.dev/blog`.\n\n![Firecrawl blog homepage showing multiple blog posts that will be crawled and collected](/images/blog/scraper-vs-crawler/firecrawl_blog.webp)\n\n### The traditional approach and its problems\n\nA standard crawling workflow would look like this: fetch the blog index, parse all the post links, add them to a queue, visit each one, extract the content, handle pagination if there's more than one index page, and repeat. You'd also need to deduplicate URLs (the same post might be linked from multiple places), respect rate limits, and handle failed requests gracefully.\n\nThat's a lot of infrastructure before you've extracted a single piece of content. Most of your code ends up managing the queue and retry logic rather than doing anything useful with the data.\n\n### How Firecrawl handles this\n\nFirecrawl's [crawl endpoint](https://docs.firecrawl.dev/features/crawl) handles discovery, deduplication, and rate limiting automatically. You give it a starting URL, set boundaries (like staying within `/blog/*`), and it returns all the pages it finds. Combine this with the `JsonFormat` extraction we used earlier, and you get structured metadata plus full markdown content for every post in one pass.\n\n### Defining the schema\n\nSince we're requesting the `markdown` format separately, the extraction schema only needs the metadata fields:\n\n```python\nfrom firecrawl import FirecrawlApp\nfrom firecrawl.v2.types import ScrapeOptions, JsonFormat\nfrom pydantic import BaseModel\nfrom dotenv import load_dotenv\nimport json\nimport os\n\nload_dotenv()\n\napp = FirecrawlApp(api_key=os.getenv(\"FIRECRAWL_API_KEY\"))\n\n\nclass BlogPost(BaseModel):\n    title: str\n    date: str\n```\n\n### Crawling with extraction\n\nOne call handles both discovery and extraction:\n\n```python\nresult = app.crawl(\n    \"https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog\",\n    limit=100,\n    include_paths=[\"/blog/*\"],\n    scrape_options=ScrapeOptions(\n        formats=[\n            \"markdown\",\n            JsonFormat(\n                type=\"json\",\n                prompt=\"Extract the blog post title and publication date.\",\n                schema=BlogPost.model_json_schema(),\n            )\n        ]\n    ),\n    poll_interval=5\n)\n\nprint(f\"Crawled {len(result.data)} pages\")\n```\n\n```plaintext\nCrawled 101 pages\n```\n\nThe `include_paths` parameter keeps the crawler within the blog section. Without it, the crawler would follow links to documentation, pricing pages, and anywhere else linked from the blog. The `limit` caps total pages to prevent runaway crawls.\n\nUnlike `scrape_url` which returns instantly, `crawl` runs asynchronously. The `poll_interval` parameter tells the SDK to check for completion every 5 seconds and return once the job finishes.\n\n### Processing the results\n\nEach page in `result.data` contains the extracted JSON, markdown content, and metadata:\n\n```python\nposts = []\nfor page in result.data:\n    json_data = page.json or {}\n    if json_data.get(\"title\"):\n        posts.append({\n            \"title\": json_data.get(\"title\"),\n            \"url\": page.metadata.source_url if page.metadata else None,\n            \"date\": json_data.get(\"date\"),\n            \"content\": page.markdown or \"\"\n        })\n\nwith open(\"firecrawl_blog_posts.json\", \"w\") as f:\n    json.dump(posts, f, indent=2)\n\nprint(f\"Saved {len(posts)} blog posts\")\n```\n\n```plaintext\nSaved 99 blog posts\n```\n\n### Sample output\n\n```python\nfor post in posts[:3]:\n    print(f\"\\n{post['title']}\")\n    print(f\"  Date: {post['date']}\")\n    print(f\"  URL: {post['url']}\")\n```\n\n```plaintext\n11 AI Agent Projects You Can Build Today (With Guides)\n  Date: Sep 18, 2025\n  URL: https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/11-ai-agent-projects\n\nLaunch Week II - Day 5: Announcing New Actions\n  Date: Nov 01, 2024\n  URL: https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/launch-week-ii-day-5-introducing-two-new-actions\n\nHow to Build a Client Relationship Tree Visualization Tool in Python\n  Date: Mar 07, 2025\n  URL: https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/client-relationship-tree-visualization-in-python\n```\n\nNinety-nine blog posts discovered and extracted from a single API call. No manual link parsing, no queue management, no pagination handling. The scraping section pulled 62 jobs from one page we already knew about. This section collected content from an entire blog without knowing any of the individual URLs upfront. That's the difference between scraping and crawling: targeted extraction versus discovery at scale.\n\nFor advanced crawling patterns, see [mastering the crawl endpoint](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/mastering-the-crawl-endpoint-in-firecrawl).\n\n## Popular tools compared for scraping and crawling\n\nThe scraping and crawling ecosystem splits into three categories: libraries, frameworks, and API services.\n\n| Tool                                                                                | Type               | Scraping | Crawling | JS Rendering  | Best For                    |\n| ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------ | -------- | -------- | ------------- | --------------------------- |\n| [BeautifulSoup](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/beautifulsoup4-vs-scrapy-comparison) | Library (Python)   | Yes      | No       | No            | Simple HTML parsing         |\n| [Scrapy](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/beautifulsoup4-vs-scrapy-comparison)        | Framework (Python) | Yes      | Yes      | Plugin needed | Large-scale projects        |\n| Playwright                                                                          | Library (Multi)    | Yes      | Manual   | Yes           | Dynamic pages, testing      |\n| Crawlee                                                                             | Framework (JS/TS)  | Yes      | Yes      | Yes           | Modern web crawling         |\n| Firecrawl                                                                           | API                | Yes      | Yes      | Yes           | LLM extraction, quick setup |\n\nLibraries give you the most control but require the most code. You handle requests, parsing, and storage yourself. Frameworks bundle common patterns (request queues, retry logic, data pipelines) so you're not rebuilding infrastructure for every project. API services trade control for speed: you send requests, they handle the infrastructure, you pay per call.\n\nYour choice depends on three things: how much JavaScript rendering you need, whether you want to manage infrastructure, and how often site layouts change.\n\n- If you're scraping static HTML from a handful of pages, BeautifulSoup and requests get the job done.\n- If you need to crawl thousands of pages with complex logic, Scrapy or Crawlee give you the scaffolding.\n- If you want structured data without writing selectors that break on every redesign, an API like [Firecrawl](https://www.firecrawl.dev/) with LLM extraction saves maintenance time.\n\nEven if you don't want to write code to build you web scrapers or crawlers, you can use no-code tools like [n8n](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/n8n-web-scraping-workflow-templates) and [Lovable](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/firecrawl-lovable-integration) that have native Firecrawl integrations!\n\n*P.S: Checkout how you can [build AI-powered apps with Lovable and Firecrawl](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/firecrawl-lovable-tutorial) that have access to live web data*\n\n## Conclusion\n\nScrapers and crawlers solve different problems. A scraper extracts data from pages you already know about. A crawler discovers pages first, then extracts. One targets a single URL, the other maps out a site.\n\nIn practice, most [projects](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/python-web-scraping-projects) use both. You crawl to find all the product pages, then scrape each one for prices and descriptions. You crawl a blog to discover every post, then scrape metadata and content from each. The techniques work together more often than they work alone. When your target pages contain repeating items—product grids, job listings, search results—[list crawling](/blog/list-crawling-python-beautifulsoup-scrapy-firecrawl) adds structure to the extraction step.\n\nFigure out whether you need discovery or just extraction, and the rest follows from there.\n\n## FAQs\n\n**What is the main difference between web scraping and web crawling?**\n\nScraping extracts data from specific pages you already know about. Crawling discovers pages by following links across a site. Scraping answers \"what's on this page?\" while crawling answers \"what pages exist?\"\n\n**Can I use scraping and crawling together?**\n\nYes, and most real projects do. A typical workflow crawls a site to discover all relevant URLs, then scrapes each page for structured data. The job listing and blog collector examples in this article show both patterns.\n\n**Which is faster, scraping or crawling?**\n\nScraping is faster because you're hitting known URLs directly. Crawling takes longer since it must discover pages, follow links, and handle deduplication. A single scrape call returns in seconds; a crawl job can take minutes depending on site size.\n\n**Do I need a crawler if I already have a list of URLs?**\n\nNo. If you have the URLs, just loop through them and scrape each one. Crawling is only necessary when you need to discover pages you don't know about yet.\n\n**How do scrapers handle JavaScript-heavy websites?**\n\nBasic HTTP requests only get the initial HTML, which is often empty on modern sites. Tools like Playwright run a headless browser to render [JavaScript](https://www.firecrawl.dev/glossary/web-crawling-apis/what-is-javascript-enabled-crawling) before extraction. API services like Firecrawl handle rendering automatically.\n\n**Should I build my own scraper or use an API service?**\n\nBuild your own if you need full control, have specific requirements, or want to avoid per-request costs. Use an API service if you want faster setup, don't want to maintain infrastructure, or need features like LLM extraction without writing selector logic.\n"])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"a6:Tadd9,"])</script><script>self.__next_f.push([1,"\n## Introduction\n\nLast year, [Llama 4](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/fine-tuning-llama4-custom-dataset-firecrawl) came out with 10 million tokens of context window. Naturally, people started wondering if that was the end of RAG because the models seem to crush needle-in-a haystack-benchmarks, where information needs to be retrieved from vast amounts of data.\n\nWell, setting aside the fact that Meta [half-cheated in those benchmarks](https://youtu.be/P4M9wfJH-yI?si=eq_QFTWlulN1tC3f), not everybody is going to RAG with Llama 4 family as they don't fit on any consumer GPU.\n\nIt's 2026 and RAG is still very much relevant, as it is the best technique to enhance LLM's capabilities regardless of their size and context window. This article explores the top open-source RAG frameworks available today, highlighting their unique features, strengths, and how they can be integrated into your AI applications.\n\nOnce you've chosen a framework, optimizing your [chunking strategy](/blog/best-chunking-strategies-rag) becomes crucial for retrieval performance. For enhanced data retrieval in your RAG pipeline, check out our guide on [MCP servers for Cursor](/blog/best-mcp-servers-for-cursor) which can provide external data sources. Also see how to prepare website data with our [llms.txt file creation guide](/blog/How-to-Create-an-llms-txt-File-for-Any-Website).\n\n## Firecrawl: the Best Friend of All RAG Frameworks\n\nBefore we dive into the most popular open-source RAG frameworks, let's take a moment to discuss [Firecrawl](https://firecrawl.dev/).\n\nTo build great RAG pipelines, you need high-quality datasets. However, your existing knowledge base may often lack information in certain areas requiring you to enrich it with open web data.\n\nThis is where [Firecrawl](https://firecrawl.dev) is your new best friend - it is the best AI-powered scraping engine to collect LLM-friendly web data at scale.\n\nPerhaps, its most useful feature for RAG projects is its [LLMs.txt generation](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/How-to-Create-an-llms-txt-File-for-Any-Website), where Firecrawl dumps entire websites (regardless of their size) into a single text file in a few lines of code:\n\n```python\nfrom firecrawl import Firecrawl\n\nfirecrawl = Firecrawl(api_key=\"fc-YOUR-API-KEY\")\n\n# Scrape a website:\ndoc = firecrawl.scrape(\"https://firecrawl.dev\", formats=[\"markdown\", \"html\"])\nprint(doc)\n```\n\nIf that't not what you need, Firecrawl offers other features like:\n\n- [Crawl \u0026 scrape method](https://docs.firecrawl.dev/features/crawl) to traverse websites, converting each page it visits to Markdown for easy LLM consumption\n- [Agent](https://docs.firecrawl.dev/features/agent) where you describe the data you need and let the AI agent find and extract it for you\n- [Deep research endpoint](https://docs.firecrawl.dev/features/alpha/deep-research) for adding OpenAI-like deep research capabilities to your RAG pipelines\n\nEach of these methods work with Firecrawl's built-in anti-bot measures and proxy rotation, leaving you to focus on the data collection process itself rather than worrying about the code.\n\nFor a practical demonstration of Firecrawl in action, explore our detailed articles that walk through creating custom datasets and building AI applications:\n\n- [Fine-tuning Gemma 3 with Firecrawl and Unsloth](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/gemma-3-fine-tuning-firecrawl-unsloth)\n- [Creating custom instruction datasets for LLM fine-tuning](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/custom-instruction-datasets-llm-fine-tuning)\n- [Fine-tuning DeepSeek models](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/fine-tuning-deepseek)\n- [Converting websites to AI agents with Firecrawl and OpenAI](https://www.firecrawl.dev/blog/website-to-agent-with-firecrawl-openai)\n- [Building production agents with leading frameworks](/blog/best-open-source-agent-frameworks-2025)\n- [Automating web data collection with n8n workflows](/blog/n8n-web-scraping-workflow-templates)\n\nWhichever framework you choose from the list below, Firecrawl can serve as a powerful data collection companion to ensure your RAG system has access to the most relevant and up-to-date information from across the web.\n\n_Note: If you want to jump to the decision table comparing all frameworks, [read the last section](#decision-table-for-choosing-the-right-rag-framework) at the end of this notebook._\n\n## Leading Open-Source RAG Frameworks\n\n### 1. [LangChain](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain) - ⭐️105k\n\n![LangChain logo - open-source framework for building LLM applications and RAG systems](/images/blog/os-rag-frameworks/langchain.webp)\n\nLangChain emerged as one of the earlier frameworks for building LLM-powered applications and has established a significant presence in the RAG ecosystem. It provides a framework for chaining together components and integrations to develop AI applications while adapting to evolving technology. With interfaces for models, embeddings, and vector stores, LangChain offers a structured approach to implementing retrieval-augmented generation systems.\n\nLangChain includes several features relevant to RAG implementations:\n\n- **Data connection** - Links LLMs to various data sources and systems through integration libraries\n- **Model flexibility** - Allows swapping between different models as requirements change\n- **Integration options** - Supports various model providers, tools, vector stores, and retrievers\n- **Retrieval components** - Enables building retrieval pipelines with different strategies (for optimizing these pipelines, see our guide on [chunking strategies for RAG](/blog/best-chunking-strategies-rag))\n- **Evaluation tools** - Provides methods to test and measure RAG system performance\n- **Ecosystem compatibility** - Works with [LangSmith](https://smith.langchain.com/) for debugging and [LangGraph](https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/) for workflow management\n\nLangChain can be installed using `pip install -U langchain`.\n\n### 2. [Dify](https://github.com/langgenius/dify) - ⭐️90.5k\n\n![Dify platform interface showing the visual workflow builder and RAG capabilities for LLM application development](/images/blog/os-rag-frameworks/dify.webp)\n\nDify is an open-source LLM application development platform that combines visual workflow building with powerful RAG capabilities. Its intuitive interface eliminates the need for extensive coding, making it accessible to both developers and non-technical users. With comprehensive support for document ingestion, retrieval, and agent orchestration, Dify provides an end-to-end solution for building production-ready AI applications.\n\nDify offers a range of features that make it a versatile tool for RAG implementations:\n\n- **Visual workflow editor** - Build and test AI workflows on a visual canvas without writing code\n- **Extensive model support** - Integrate with hundreds of proprietary and open-source LLMs from various providers\n- **RAG pipeline** - Process documents from ingestion to retrieval with support for PDFs, PPTs, and other formats\n- **Agent capabilities** - Create agents with LLM Function Calling or ReAct, with 50+ built-in tools\n- **LLMOps** - Monitor application logs and performance metrics to improve prompts and models\n- **Backend-as-a-Service** - Use APIs to integrate Dify capabilities into existing business applications\n- **Enterprise features** - Access SSO, role-based access control, and other organization-focused capabilities\n\nGetting started with Dify is straightforward using Docker Compose. Simply clone the repository, navigate to the docker directory, create an environment file from the example, and run `docker compose up -d`.\n\nAfter deployment, access the dashboard at `http://localhost/install` to begin the initialization process.\n\nFor detailed installation instructions, visit the [official documentation](https://docs.dify.ai/getting-started/install-self-hosted).\n\n### 3. [RAGFlow](https://github.com/infiniflow/ragflow) - ⭐️48.5k\n\n![RAGFlow logo showing the official branding for the open-source RAG engine with deep document understanding capabilities](/images/blog/os-rag-frameworks/ragflow.webp)\n\nRAGFlow is an open-source RAG engine built around deep document understanding capabilities. Unlike many other RAG frameworks, it excels at extracting structured information from complex documents like PDFs, including tables, layouts, and visual elements. With its comprehensive document parsing system and intuitive web interface, RAGFlow streamlines the entire process from document ingestion to generation.\n\nRAGFlow offers powerful features designed for advanced document-based retrieval:\n\n- **Deep document understanding** - Extracts text, tables, and structure from complex documents with high fidelity\n- **Visual web interface** - Provides a user-friendly dashboard for document management and RAG workflow creation\n- **GraphRAG support** - Creates knowledge graphs from documents for more contextual retrieval\n- **Agentic reasoning** - Implements agent capabilities for more complex query resolution\n- **Multiple embedding options** - Works with various embedding models for different retrieval needs\n- **Flexible storage backends** - Supports both Elasticsearch and Infinity for document and vector storage\n- **Comprehensive API** - Offers Python SDK and REST APIs for integration with other systems\n\nGetting started with RAGFlow is straightforward using Docker. The system offers both slim (2GB) and full (9GB) Docker images depending on whether you need embedded models included. For detailed installation instructions, see the [official documentation](https://ragflow.io/docs/dev/) and the [GitHub repository](https://github.com/infiniflow/ragflow) for environment requirements and configuration options.\n\n### 4. [LlamaIndex](https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index) - ⭐️40.8k\n\n![LlamaIndex logo showing the framework's branding with a stylized llama icon representing the data framework for connecting LLMs with private data sources](https://cdn.bap-software.net/2024/05/27174818/LlamaIndex-e1716781781228.png)\n\nLlamaIndex is a comprehensive data framework designed to connect LLMs with private data sources, making it a powerful foundation for building RAG applications. It provides a structured approach to data ingestion, indexing, and retrieval that simplifies the development of knowledge-augmented AI systems. With its modular architecture, LlamaIndex bridges the gap between raw data and LLM capabilities, enabling contextual reasoning over custom datasets.\n\nKey features of LlamaIndex include:\n\n- **Flexible data connectors** - Ingest data from various sources and formats including APIs, PDFs, documents, and SQL databases\n- **Customizable indexing** - Structure data efficiently using vector stores, keyword indices, or knowledge graphs\n- **Advanced retrieval mechanisms** - Implement sophisticated query engines with contextual relevance\n- **Modular architecture** - Mix and match components to create tailored RAG pipelines\n- **Multi-modal support** - Work with text, images, and other data types in unified workflows\n- **Extensive integration ecosystem** - Over 300 integration packages to work with preferred LLMs, embeddings, and vector stores\n- **Optimization tools** - Fine-tune retrieval performance through reranking and response synthesis techniques\n\nGetting started with LlamaIndex offers two approaches: using the starter package (`pip install llama-index`) which includes core functionality and common integrations, or a customized installation beginning with the core (`pip install llama-index-core`) and adding specific integration packages as needed. Basic usage requires only a few lines of code to ingest documents, create an index, and build a query engine to retrieve information from your data.\n\n### 5. [Milvus](https://github.com/milvus-io/milvus) - ⭐️33.9k\n\n![Milvus logo showing the official branding for the high-performance vector database designed for similarity search and AI applications](/images/blog/os-rag-frameworks/milvus.webp)\n\nMilvus is a high-performance, cloud-native vector database built specifically for scalable vector similarity search. As a cornerstone technology for RAG applications, it enables efficient storage and retrieval of embedding vectors generated from text, images, or other unstructured data. Milvus provides optimized search algorithms that balance speed and accuracy, making it particularly valuable for production-grade RAG systems handling large volumes of data.\n\nMilvus offers several key features that enhance RAG implementations:\n\n- **Advanced vector search** - Supports multiple ANN (Approximate Nearest Neighbor) algorithms for optimal vector similarity matching\n- **Hybrid search capabilities** - Combines vector similarity with scalar filtering and full-text search\n- **Horizontal scalability** - Handles billions of vectors across distributed clusters\n- **Multi-modal support** - Works with embeddings from text, images, video, and other data types\n- **Rich query options** - Provides distance metrics, search parameters, and result filtering\n- **Seamless integration** - Works with LangChain, LlamaIndex, and other RAG frameworks. For a comprehensive comparison of vector database options, see our [best vector databases 2026](/blog/best-vector-databases-2025) guide\n- **Enterprise features** - Includes data consistency guarantees, access controls, and monitoring tools\n- **Specialized RAG optimizations** - Offers features like multi-vector search for advanced retrieval techniques\n\nGetting started with Milvus is straightforward with Docker. You can run a standalone instance with a single command (`docker run -d --name milvus -p 19530:19530 -p 9091:9091 milvusdb/milvus:latest`), then use Python client libraries to interact with it. For detailed installation instructions, see the [Docker installation guide](https://milvus.io/docs/install_standalone-docker.md). The [quickstart documentation](https://milvus.io/docs/quickstart.md) provides code examples for creating collections, inserting vectors, and performing searches, while the [RAG tutorials](https://milvus.io/docs/build-rag-with-milvus.md) offer end-to-end implementation guidance.\n\n### 6. [mem0](https://github.com/mem0ai/mem0) - ⭐️27.3k\n\n![Mem0 logo banner showing the intelligent memory layer for AI applications with its distinctive branding and design](https://github.com/mem0ai/mem0/raw/main/docs/images/banner-sm.png)\n\nMem0 (pronounced \"mem-zero\") is an intelligent memory layer designed to enhance RAG applications with persistent, contextual memory capabilities. Unlike traditional RAG frameworks that focus primarily on document retrieval, mem0 enables AI systems to actively learn from and adapt to user interactions over time. By combining LLMs with specialized vector storage, mem0 creates AI assistants that can maintain user preferences, conversation history, and important information across multiple sessions.\n\nMem0 offers powerful features to enhance RAG implementations:\n\n- **Multi-level memory architecture** - Maintains user, session, and agent memory for comprehensive context retention\n- **Automatic memory processing** - Uses LLMs to extract and store important information from conversations\n- **Memory management** - Continuously updates stored information and resolves contradictions to maintain accuracy\n- **Dual storage architecture** - Combines vector database for memory storage with graph database for relationship tracking\n- **Smart retrieval system** - Employs semantic search and graph queries to find relevant memories based on importance and recency\n- **Simple API integration** - Provides easy-to-use endpoints for adding and retrieving memories\n- **Cross-platform support** - Available for both Python and Node.js applications\n\nGetting started with mem0 is straightforward, with two main options: a fully managed platform for hassle-free deployment or self-hosting using the open-source package. For self-hosting, simply install via `pip install mem0ai` (Python) or `npm install mem0ai` (Node.js) and initialize with just a few lines of code. The basic implementation requires configuring an LLM (with GPT-4o-mini as the default) and implementing memory retrieval and storage functions. Comprehensive documentation, examples, and integration guides are available at the [official documentation site](https://docs.mem0.ai/overview).\n\n### 7. [DSPy](https://github.com/stanfordnlp/dspy) - ⭐️23k\n\n![DSPy logo showing Stanford NLP's framework for programming language models with a minimalist design featuring the DSPy name](https://github.com/stanfordnlp/dspy/raw/main/docs/docs/static/img/dspy_logo.png)\n\nDSPy is a framework for programming—rather than prompting—language models, developed by Stanford NLP. Unlike traditional RAG tools that rely on fixed prompts, DSPy enables developers to create modular, self-improving retrieval systems through declarative Python code. Its unique approach allows for systematic optimization of both prompts and weights in RAG pipelines, resulting in more reliable and higher-quality outputs than manual prompt engineering alone.\n\nDSPy offers a powerful set of capabilities for building advanced RAG applications:\n\n- **Modular architecture** - Build composable AI systems with reusable, purpose-specific components\n- **Automatic prompt optimization** - Leverage optimizers like MIPROv2 to systematically improve prompts rather than manual tuning\n- **Multiple retrieval integrations** - Connect to various vector databases including Milvus, Chroma, FAISS, and many others\n- **Evaluation framework** - Test and measure RAG system performance with built-in metrics\n- **Compiler approach** - Transform declarative language model calls into self-improving pipelines\n- **Flexible pipeline design** - Support for various RAG approaches from basic to multi-hop and complex reasoning\n- **Production readiness** - Tools for debugging, deployment, and observability\n\nGetting started with DSPy is straightforward with installation via `pip install dspy`. The framework provides a clear programming model for defining signatures (input/output specifications) and modules (components that implement those signatures) for your RAG system. DSPy's optimization capabilities can then automatically improve your implementation based on example data. For comprehensive documentation and tutorials, visit the [official documentation site](https://dspy.ai/), and specifically check out the [RAG-focused tutorial](https://dspy.ai/tutorials/rag/) to build your first retrieval-augmented generation system.\n\n### 8. [Haystack](https://github.com/deepset-ai/haystack) - ⭐️20.2k\n\n![Haystack logo banner showing the modular LLM framework with its distinctive blue and white design for building AI applications](/images/blog/os-rag-frameworks/haystack.webp)\n\nHaystack is an end-to-end AI orchestration framework designed for building flexible, production-ready LLM applications. It excels at retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) implementations by providing a modular component architecture that connects models, vector databases, and file converters into customizable pipelines or agents. With its technology-agnostic approach, Haystack allows developers to easily switch between different models and databases without rewriting their applications, making it ideal for building sophisticated RAG systems that evolve with changing requirements.\n\nHaystack offers a robust set of features for implementing advanced RAG solutions:\n\n- **Flexible component system** - Build pipelines by connecting reusable components for document processing, retrieval, and generation\n- **Technology-agnostic approach** - Use models from OpenAI, Cohere, Hugging Face, or custom models hosted on various platforms\n- **Advanced retrieval methods** - Implement sophisticated search strategies beyond basic vector similarity\n- **Document processing** - Convert, clean, and split various file formats for effective indexing\n- **Evaluation frameworks** - Test and benchmark your RAG pipelines to measure performance\n- **Customization options** - Create custom components when standard behavior doesn't meet your requirements\n- **Visual pipeline builder** - Design pipelines visually with deepset Studio integration\n\nGetting started with Haystack is straightforward with installation via `pip install haystack-ai`. The framework provides comprehensive [documentation](https://docs.haystack.deepset.ai/docs/intro) with guides for building your first LLM application in minutes. The [installation guide](https://docs.haystack.deepset.ai/docs/installation) covers multiple methods including Docker, while the [get started guide](https://docs.haystack.deepset.ai/docs/get_started) walks through basic pipeline creation. For more advanced use cases, you can explore the [cookbook](https://haystack.deepset.ai/tutorials) with recipes for various RAG implementations.\n\n### 9. [LightRAG](https://github.com/HKUDS/LightRAG) - ⭐️14.6k\n\n![LightRAG architecture diagram showing the streamlined approach to retrieval-augmented generation with its key components and workflow](https://github.com/HKUDS/LightRAG/raw/main/README.assets/b2aaf634151b4706892693ffb43d9093.png)\n\nLightRAG is a streamlined approach to retrieval-augmented generation that focuses on simplicity and performance. As indicated by its name, it offers a lightweight implementation that delivers faster and more efficient RAG capabilities compared to more complex alternatives. Based on benchmark results shown in the repository, LightRAG consistently outperforms several other RAG methodologies across multiple evaluation dimensions, making it particularly valuable for applications requiring both speed and quality.\n\nLightRAG provides several notable features for effective RAG implementation:\n\n- **Performance optimization** - Delivers superior results compared to traditional RAG approaches in benchmark tests\n- **Simple architecture** - Maintains a straightforward implementation that's easier to deploy and maintain\n- **Comprehensive retrieval** - Excels at extracting relevant information from document contexts\n- **Information diversity** - Retrieves varied and representative content rather than redundant information\n- **User empowerment** - Provides capabilities that enable more effective information access\n- **Web interface** - Includes a web UI component for interactive exploration and usage\n- **Bulk processing** - Efficiently handles insertion and processing of multiple documents\n\nGetting started with LightRAG involves installing the package and setting up your document processing pipeline. The repository provides example code for extracting contexts, inserting them into the system, generating queries, and retrieving relevant information. The codebase includes a comprehensive set of reproduction scripts that demonstrate the core functionality. For more technical details and implementation guidance, refer to the [GitHub repository](https://github.com/HKUDS/LightRAG) and its associated documentation.\n\n### 10. [LLMWare](https://github.com/llmware-ai/llmware) - ⭐️12.7k\n\nLLMWare is a unified framework designed specifically for building enterprise-grade RAG pipelines using small, specialized models rather than exclusively relying on massive LLMs. This approach delivers more efficient and cost-effective RAG implementations that can often run on standard hardware, including laptops. With its comprehensive document processing capabilities and flexible architecture, LLMWare enables organizations to implement production-ready RAG systems that balance performance with resource efficiency.\n\nLLMWare provides a robust set of features for building specialized RAG applications:\n\n- **Efficient model deployment** - Utilize smaller, specialized models that can run on CPUs and edge devices\n- **Comprehensive document processing** - Handle various file formats including PDFs, Office documents, text, and markdown\n- **Multiple vector database options** - Integrate with MongoDB, Postgres, SQLite, PG Vector, Redis, Qdrant, and Milvus\n- **Diverse embedding models** - Support for 10+ embedding models including nomic, jina, bge, gte, ember, and OpenAI\n- **Parallelized parsing** - Process large document collections efficiently with parallelized operations\n- **Dual pass retrieval** - Enhance retrieval quality with sophisticated query techniques\n- **Document summarization** - Generate document summaries as part of the processing pipeline\n- **GPU acceleration** - Leverage GPU resources when available for model inferencing\n\nGetting started with LLMWare is straightforward with installation via `pip install llmware`. The framework provides a collection of example scripts in the [Getting_Started directory](https://github.com/llmware-ai/llmware/tree/main/examples/Getting_Started) that demonstrate core capabilities like document parsing, embedding generation, and retrieval. Additional examples show how to work with specific models like [Qwen2](https://github.com/llmware-ai/llmware/blob/main/examples/Models/using-qwen2-models.py) and create complete RAG pipelines. The repository also includes detailed documentation and fast-start scripts for quickly implementing common workflows.\n\n### 11.
